title: Overview
text: Overview This wiki currently tracks 175 source pages and 1602 extracted keywords. Current Coverage Variations in definitions used for describing restrictive care practices (seclusion and restraint) in adult mental health inpatient units a systematic: There is significant variation in how restrictive care practices are defined across recent literature, undermining the validity of cross-study comparisons and prevalence reporting. The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention: The article argues that current U.S. mental health law unjustly denies people with psychosocial disabilities the right to refuse treatment and access culturally responsive care, violating constitutional and international human rights protections. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint; Prioritizing Patient Experience: The ethical analysis of chemical restraints must prioritize patient lived experience to address how psychotropic drugs undermine authentic consent and perpetuate epistemic injustice through drug-induced compliance and systemic biases like sanism. The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint Prioritizing Patient Experience: Ethical analysis of chemical restraint must prioritize patient experience and account for how psychotropic drugs alter psychological states, which can undermine authentic consent even when decisional capacity appears restored. The Limitations of Principlism: Principlism is inadequate for ethica

title: Six Core Strategies
text: Six Core Strategies What It Is The Six Core Strategies is a framework designed to reduce the use of seclusion and restraint in mental health settings. It emphasizes leadership, data use, workforce development, prevention tools, service user involvement, and debriefing to promote safer, more therapeutic environments. Its application supports systemic change by addressing both organizational practices and relational dynamics in inpatient care. Role in the Literature In 2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings: Referenced as a foundational framework linked to Safewards, informing practice implications for restraint reduction. In 2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care: Described as a structured approach guiding implementation through leadership, data use, workforce development, prevention tools, service user roles, and debriefing. In 2024 - Savage et al. - Comparison of coercive practices in worldwide mental healthcare; overcoming difficulties resulting from variations in monitoring strategies: Cited as an evidence-based framework supporting debriefing, leadership involvement, and data-driven change to reduce coercion globally. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12: Identified as one of three models helping institutions enhance relational safety by targeting leadership, data, workforc

title: Safewards
text: Safewards What It Is Safewards is an evidence-based model designed to reduce conflict and containment in psychiatric inpatient settings through staff training, environmental modifications, and relational safety interventions. It promotes alternatives to restrictive practices by enhancing safety and communication between patients and staff. The model matters in mental health literature as a recommended approach to minimizing coercion and improving care quality. Role in the Literature In 2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings: Model promoted to reduce restrictive practices; cited as a recommended alternative. In 2020 - Muir-Cochrane - A wicked problem Chemical restraint towards a definition: referenced as an evidence-based, least restrictive approach to reducing coercive practices. In 2020 - Rodrigues - Implementing Alternatives to Coercion; A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care: An evidence-based model designed to reduce conflict and containment in psychiatric wards through staff training and environmental modifications. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12: presented as another model promoting relational safety through specific interventions like mutual help, clear boundaries, and de-escalation to reduce conflict and containment in mental health wards.

title: psychiatric ethics
text: psychiatric ethics Why It Matters Ethical frameworks guide clinical decisions in psychiatric care where coercion, consent, and capacity intersect. Cultural and contextual variation challenges universal application of ethical principles in global psychiatry. Emerging issues like chemical restraints require re-evaluation of autonomy and liberty in treatment settings. International efforts aim to harmonize ethical standards through shared guidelines and lived experience integration. title: Mental Capacity Act 2005
text: Mental Capacity Act 2005 What It Is The Mental Capacity Act 2005 is a UK law that provides a legal framework for decision-making on behalf of individuals who lack the mental capacity to make specific decisions for themselves. It establishes principles for assessing capacity, making best-interest decisions, and safeguarding the rights of vulnerable adults in health and social care contexts. It is central to ethical and legal discussions about autonomy and paternalism in clinical practice. Role in the Literature In 2008 - Lepping - Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions: Legislation supporting decision-making for those who may lack capacity, central to the rights-focused framework and capacity assessment requirements. title: treatment gap
text: treatment gap Why It Matters The concept of treatment gap highlights systemic failures in mental health care delivery, particularly the lack of access to effective treatment for severe conditions. It underscores how critiques of psychiatry that ignore treatment gaps may misattribute human rights violations to biomedical approaches rather than systemic under-resourcing. Addressing treatment gaps is essential for evidence-based care and reducing preventable suffering in mental health systems. Recognizing treatment gaps supports arguments for increasing investment in mental health services as a human rights imperative. title: Joint Commission

title: PRISMA
text: PRISMA What It Is PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) is an evidence-based minimum set of items for reporting in systematic reviews and meta-analyses. It standardizes the reporting process to enhance transparency, reproducibility, and methodological rigor in research synthesis. Role in the Literature In 2022 - Butterworth et al. - Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis: Framework followed for transparent reporting of the systematic review process, enhancing reproducibility and minimizing bias in study selection and synthesis. In 2024 - Parsaei et al. - Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders a systematic review: The reporting standard adhered to for ensuring transparency in the systematic review process.

title: recovery model

title: PROSPERO
text: PROSPERO What It Is PROSPERO (International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews) is an international database for registering systematic review protocols in health and social care. It promotes transparency, reduces duplication, and supports evidence-based practice by making protocols publicly accessible before review commencement. Role in the Literature In 2019 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - International research into 22 years of use of chemical restraint An evidence overview: hosted the protocol registration (ID: CRD 42017055258) ensuring transparency and reducing duplication. In 2020 - Bruijn et al. - Physical and Pharmacological Restraints in Hospital Care; Protocol for a Systematic Review: international prospective register of systematic reviews where this protocol was registered (CRD42019116186). In 2020 - Muir-Cochrane et al. - Prevalence of the use of chemical restraint in the management of challenging behaviours associated with adult mental health conditions A: trial registration system where the systematic review protocol (CRD42017055258) was registered. In 2022 - Butterworth et al. - Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis: international prospective register of systematic reviews where the review protocol was pre-registered (CRD42020176859), ensuring transparency and reducing duplication in research.

title: European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR)
text: European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) What It Is The European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) is an international treaty established by the Council of Europe to protect human rights and fundamental freedoms in Europe. It forms the legal foundation for the European Court of Human Rights and is central to judicial interpretations of dignity, liberty, and private life in mental health and capacity contexts. Role in the Literature In 2018 - Cusack et al. - An integrative review exploring the physical and psychological harm inherent in using restraint in mental health inpatient settings: Cited regarding Articles 3 (inhumane treatment), 5 (liberty), and 8 (private life) in relation to restraint practices. In Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -05: Discussed for its lack of explicit dignity reference but judicial affirmation in cases like Pretty v UK.

title: Supported Decision-Making
text: Supported Decision-Making Why It Matters Supported decision-making is essential for upholding the rights and dignity of persons with disabilities under international human rights law It provides a framework that preserves legal capacity while recognizing the need for support in decision-making It contrasts with substitute decision-making, emphasizing autonomy and avoiding paternalistic interventions It aligns with the indivisibility of human rights, ensuring that support does not compromise safety or personhood Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Supported decision-making as a right | Article 12 of the CRPD mandates supported decision-making to uphold legal capacity, dignity, and safety, with substitute decision-making only as a last resort in hard cases | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -01 | | Balance of social model and embodiment | Effective disability rights frameworks must integrate social model insights with recognition of embodied impairment, especially for cognitive disability, to avoid undermining autonomy | Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights -02 | Sub-topics supported-decision-making (note: this is the current page; no sub-topics exist in the provided list that are more specific and distinct) Related concepts decision-making-capacity substitute-decision-making substituted-decision-making substituted-judgment legal-capacity autonomy relat

title: QT prolongation
text: QT prolongation Why It Matters QT prolongation is a critical cardiac risk associated with certain psychiatric medications It increases risk of torsades de pointes, a potentially fatal arrhythmia Relevant in acute agitation treatment where antipsychotics like haloperidol are commonly used Monitoring is essential when using chemical restraints in emergency psychiatric settings Key Positions | Position | Core claim | Key sources | |---------|------------|-------------| | Haloperidol use carries QT prolongation risk | Haloperidol, though preferred for acute agitation, poses cardiac risks including QT prolongation requiring monitoring | 2019 - Zareifopoulos et al. - Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence | | Benzodiazepines avoid QT effects but have other risks | Benzodiazepines are less effective for agitation and carry risks like respiratory depression but do not typically cause QT prolongation | 2019 - Zareifopoulos et al. - Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients Theoretical and Empirical Evidence | | Chemical restraints require cardiac risk assessment | Use of chemical restraints necessitates evaluation of QT prolongation and other cardiac risks per safety protocols | 2020 - Guerrero et al. - Physical and Chemical Restraints (an Update) | | Monitoring protocols mitigate QT risk | Proper documentation and monitoring during chemical restraint use help identify and manage QT prolongation early | 2020

title: European Convention on Human Rights
text: European Convention on Human Rights What It Is The European Convention on Human Rights is an international treaty designed to protect fundamental human rights and freedoms across Europe, forming the basis of legal standards interpreted by the European Court of Human Rights. It establishes binding obligations on member states to uphold civil and political rights, including prohibitions on torture, arbitrary detention, and inhuman treatment. In scholarly literature, it serves as a critical framework for analyzing the limits and enforcement of state power, particularly regarding absolute rights and detention practices. Role in the Literature In 2016 - Webber - Proportionality and Absolute Rights: The Convention's provisions, particularly Article 3 on torture, are analyzed for being treated as absolute prohibitions in case law despite formal limitation mechanisms elsewhere. In Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 12: Article 5 is referenced in discussions of detention of individuals deemed "of unsound mind," with the European Court interpreting such measures as potentially violating the Convention in mental health contexts.

title: Respiratory depression
text: Respiratory depression Why It Matters It is a serious adverse effect of certain pharmacological interventions used in acute agitation management. Monitoring for respiratory depression is critical to patient safety during rapid tranquilization. It influences risk-benefit assessments when selecting medications for behavioral emergencies. It is a key consideration in avoiding harm and upholding the principle of non-maleficence. title: Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health
text: Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Right of Everyone to the Enjoyment of the Highest Attainable Standard of Physical and Mental Health Summary The report critically examines global mental health systems through a human rights framework, arguing that the dominance of the biomedical model has led to systemic neglect, coercion, and human rights violations, particularly against persons with psychosocial, intellectual, and cognitive disabilities. It emphasizes that mental health cannot be reduced to neurobiological factors alone and must integrate psychological, social, and economic dimensions. Drawing on data from the WHO Mental Health Atlas 2014, the report highlights chronic underfunding, with less than 7% of global health budgets allocated to mental health and less than $2 per person annually in low-income countries. It critiques the overreliance on psychotropic medications, noting limited efficacy and significant side effects, while pointing to growing evidence supporting psychosocial and recovery-oriented alternatives. The report positions the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) as a transformative legal instrument that redefines state obligations, particularly regarding legal capacity, informed consent, and the prohibition of forced treatment. It calls for a paradigm shift toward community-based, rights-respecting systems that prioritize participation, non-discrimination, and accountability, while addressing root causes such as poverty, violenc

title: The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care
text: The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care Summary This chapter examines the use of mechanical restraint in forensic psychiatric settings, focusing on legal, ethical, and clinical considerations. It analyzes how mechanical restraint—defined as the application of devices limiting patient movement for behavioral control—can be justified in high-security environments like Rampton Hospital when managing life-threatening self-injury or extreme violence. The authors draw on their experience developing a policy for restricted use, emphasizing compliance with legal frameworks such as the Mental Health Act, Mental Capacity Act, Human Rights Act, and Criminal Law Act (via the "reasonable force" and PLAN-B principles). Ethically, they apply the "four principles plus scope" framework, weighing autonomy against beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice, while acknowledging tensions with patient autonomy and potential trauma. Clinically, they note that restraint is primarily used for self-injury rather than violence in their setting, with monitoring and staff training aimed at reducing misuse, though staff discomfort persists. The chapter concludes that while evidence of efficacy is limited due to ethical barriers to research, mechanical restraint may be necessary as a last resort when governed by strict policies involving oversight, patient involvement (e.g., advance statements), and regular review. Key Entities Rampton Hospital (organization) — Site where the mechanical re

title: Preferences of Individual Mental Health Service Users Are Essential in Determining the Least Restrictive Type of Restraint
text: Preferences of Individual Mental Health Service Users Are Essential in Determining the Least Restrictive Type of Restraint Summary This commentary argues against rigid ethical hierarchies that prioritize chemical restraint over physical restraint in psychiatric settings, asserting that such rankings fail to account for divergent conceptions of basic liberty and individual patient preferences. Drawing on legal precedents from the German Federal Constitutional Court—which prioritizes bodily integrity over freedom of movement, leading to the conclusion that physical restraint may be less restrictive than involuntary medication—the authors demonstrate that ethical evaluations of restraint must be context-sensitive. Empirical evidence shows substantial variability in patient preferences regarding restraint types, with 36% of psychiatric advance directives specifying preferences for coercive measures. The authors advocate for psychiatric advance directives as a key mechanism to honor autonomy when decision-making capacity is impaired, and emphasize substituted judgment and phenomenological experience as critical factors in ethical decision-making, particularly when informed consent is unobtainable during acute psychiatric crises. Key Entities Ruhr University Bochum (type: org) — Affiliation of multiple authors; central to the research and commentary's institutional context. title: Staff Attitudes Towards Seclusion and Restraint in Forensic Settings
text: Staff Attitudes Towards Seclusion and Restraint in Forensic Settings Summary This chapter examines staff attitudes toward seclusion and restraint in forensic psychiatric settings, focusing on how these attitudes influence clinical decision-making. Drawing on cross-national survey data and qualitative studies, it identifies that staff perceptions of aggression—particularly whether it is viewed as intentional and patient-driven—play a stronger role in intervention selection than attitudes toward specific coercive methods. In forensic contexts, high exposure to violence and patient criminal histories contribute to fear and distrust among staff, which erodes therapeutic relationships and increases reliance on coercion. The authors argue that organizational safety culture in forensic settings often prioritizes control over therapeutic engagement, and that continuing education addressing both skill gaps and underlying beliefs about aggression is needed to reduce inappropriate use of restrictive practices. title: Association of surveillance technology and staff opinions with physical restraint use in nursing homes Cross-sectional study
text: Association of surveillance technology and staff opinions with physical restraint use in nursing homes Cross-sectional study Summary This cross-sectional study investigated physical restraint prevalence and its associations with surveillance technology use and staff opinions on restraint appropriateness across 86 Swiss nursing homes (2018–2019). Using multilevel logistic regression on data from 6,149 residents and 3,137 staff, the study found an overall restraint prevalence of 11.1%, with substantial unit-level variability (0.0–63.6%). Contrary to hypotheses, surveillance technologies (pressure mats, cameras, electronic door systems, bracelets) showed no significant association with reduced restraint use after adjusting for resident, unit, and facility factors. However, staff who perceived restraint use as appropriate on their unit had significantly lower odds of residents being restrained (OR: 0.48, 95% CI: 0.29–0.80), indicating that in units with higher restraint use, staff were more likely to view such use as inappropriate—potentially reflecting awareness of overuse. The findings challenge assumptions about technology as a simple alternative to restraints and highlight staff attitudes as a possible indicator for quality improvement efforts targeting overuse. title: Coercive treatment and stigma – is there a link?
text: Coercive treatment and stigma – is there a link? Summary This chapter examines the bidirectional relationship between stigma of mental illness and coercive measures in psychiatric care across European Union member states. It defines coercive measures as an umbrella term encompassing involuntary admissions, placements, seclusion, physical restraint, and forced medication, emphasizing their status as fundamental encroachments on human rights despite being inherent in psychiatric systems. The analysis draws on epidemiological data, survey research, and policy analysis to argue that stigmatizing attitudes—particularly beliefs about dangerousness and unpredictability associated with schizophrenia—correlate with public and professional support for coercive interventions. Conversely, the subjective and objective experiences of coercion (such as perceived helplessness, medication side effects, and loss of custody) reinforce negative perceptions of psychiatry and contribute to stigma. The chapter highlights significant cross-national variation in involuntary admission rates (e.g., 6 per 100,000 in Portugal vs. 215 in Finland) that cannot be fully explained by legal frameworks, suggesting cultural and attitudinal factors like stigma play a key role. It further discusses anti-stigma interventions such as Germany’s “Open the Doors” program, which demonstrated modest but significant reductions in social distance toward people with schizophrenia, indicating that stigma is modifiable and ma

title: Impact of diagnosis on outcomes for compulsory treatment orders in New Zealand
text: Impact of diagnosis on outcomes for compulsory treatment orders in New Zealand Summary This study examined whether the impact of Community Treatment Orders (CTOs) varies by psychiatric diagnosis using national linked health data from New Zealand spanning 2009–2018. Researchers analyzed data from 14,726 individuals placed under CTOs, drawing on the Programme for the Integration of Mental Health Data (PRIMHD) for demographic and service use information and the Pharmaceutical Collection for medication dispensing records. Diagnoses were hierarchically categorized using DSM-IV codes into groups including psychotic disorders, bipolar disorders, major depressive disorder, dementia, personality disorders, and others. The primary outcomes were psychiatric admission frequency and duration per year, compared during versus outside CTO periods, analyzed via rate ratios (RRs) using Poisson regression. The study found that CTOs reduced admission frequency and duration only for individuals with psychotic disorders (RR = 0.82 for admissions, RR = 0.81 for bed days), while increasing these metrics across all other diagnostic groups. Notably, depot antipsychotic dispensing rose significantly during CTOs for all groups, with the highest relative increase seen in major depressive disorder (RR = 8.34). Community contacts also increased across all diagnostic groups during CTO periods. These results suggest that CTOs do not have uniform effects but instead produce diagnosis-specific outcome patterns

title: The Criminalisation of Coercive Control; The Power of Law
text: The Criminalisation of Coercive Control; The Power of Law Summary This article examines whether criminal law has the capacity to effectively respond to coercive control in intimate partner relationships. The authors analyze how coercive control has been incorporated into legal frameworks across jurisdictions (including England and Wales, Scotland, Ireland, Tasmania, and others) and evaluate these legal responses against empirical evidence of coercive control's nature in everyday life. They argue that criminalizing coercive control fundamentally misunderstands both the nature of coercive control as a "liberty crime" involving patterned behavior rather than discrete incidents, and the coercive potential of legal systems themselves. Despite legislative innovations like the UK's Serious Crime Act 2015, enforcement remains limited (only ~9,000 recorded offenses versus 2 million domestic abuse incidents annually in England/Wales), and legal interventions produce unintended consequences including legal systems abuse, erasure of victim agency, and the masking of physical violence. The authors conclude that criminalization fails those it aims to protect and advocate for approaches that center victim autonomy and address structural gender inequalities rather than expanding criminal law. Key Entities Serious Crime Act 2015 (England and Wales) — Legislation that created the specific criminal offense of coercive control, serving as the primary case study for evaluating legal responses Dom

title: Effect of coercive measures on mental health status in adult psychiatric populations: a nationwide trial emulation
text: Effect of coercive measures on mental health status in adult psychiatric populations: a nationwide trial emulation Summary This study investigated the impact of coercive measures—seclusion, mechanical restraint, and forced medication—on mental health outcomes in adult psychiatric inpatients using nationwide Swiss hospital data from 2019 to 2021. Employing a trial emulation approach with inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW), the researchers aimed to mimic a randomized controlled trial to estimate the causal effect of these interventions on mental health status at discharge, as measured by the Health of the Nations Outcome Scale (HoNOS). The analysis included 178,369 hospitalizations after exclusions, with 9.2% involving at least one coercive measure. Results showed that exposure to any coercive measure was associated with a 1.91-point increase in HoNOS score (indicating worse mental health), with the strongest effect observed for mechanical restraint (2.83-point increase), followed by forced medication (1.97-point) and seclusion (1.60-point). All effects were statistically significant (p < .001). The study concludes that coercive measures have a detrimental impact on patients’ mental health, challenging perceptions of therapeutic benefit and underscoring the need for alternatives in clinical practice. Key Entities Swiss National Association for Quality Development in Hospitals and Clinics (ANQ) (organization): Provided the national registry data on coercive measur

title: Chemical Restraints and the Basic Liberties
text: Chemical Restraints and the Basic Liberties Summary This commentary challenges Crutchfield and Redinger's (2024) hierarchy of chemical restraints, which holds that only interventions rendering a person unconscious violate basic liberty, while those altering conscious states without unconsciousness do not. Drawing on philosophical theories of liberty (particularly Pettit's concept of "dominating control"), the author argues that intentionally altering another person's desires or mental states—such as changing their goals or values—may constitute a deeper infringement on basic liberty than mere unconsciousness. The piece uses a thought experiment involving two individuals, Bernie and Claire, to illustrate that having one's desires manipulated by another agent represents a loss of autonomy more fundamental than temporary loss of consciousness. The author distinguishes between the capacity to control one's mental states and freedom from external determination of those states, suggesting that even when individuals cannot self-regulate agitation (e.g., due to mental illness), they retain an interest in not having their mental states intentionally altered by others. The commentary calls for further ethical scrutiny of neurointerventions that modify cognition or affect, arguing they may undermine the liberty to live as a free person even without impairing consciousness. title: Law and psychiatry--current and future perspectives
text: Law and psychiatry--current and future perspectives Summary This 2022 review article examines the dynamic interface between law and psychiatry across multiple domains, including informed consent, involuntary treatment and placement, coercive measures, forensic psychiatry, digital mental health, data privacy, end-of-life decisions, and advance directives. The authors argue that legal frameworks in psychiatry are not simply imposed by law but emerge from a bidirectional, evolving relationship shaped by societal values, ethical discourse, and clinical practice. Drawing on international legal trends, empirical studies (such as those on Community Treatment Effects and German restraint regulations), and human rights frameworks like the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UN-CRPD), the paper highlights how reforms in mental health law have followed shifts toward patient autonomy, while psychiatric insights simultaneously inform legislative development. The review emphasizes regional variation and ongoing tensions—particularly between protection and liberty—and concludes that the law-psychiatry relationship will continue to evolve alongside changing societal values. Key Entities United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UN-CRPD) (type: org/treaty) — serves as a foundational human rights framework driving reform toward capacity-based mental health legislation and non-discrimination. Community Treatment Orders (CTOs) (type: tool/legal m

title: Conflict and antagonism within global psychiatry: A discourse analysis of organisational responses to the UN reports on rights-based approaches in mental health
text: Conflict and antagonism within global psychiatry: A discourse analysis of organisational responses to the UN reports on rights-based approaches in mental health Summary This study examines formal organizational responses to two United Nations Special Rapporteur reports (2017, 2020) advocating for rights-based, psychosocial approaches in mental health care. Researchers identified 13 formal responses from psychiatric associations, medical bodies, government agencies, and one joint psychology/user-survivor coalition. Using discourse theory—specifically concepts of nodal points, binary opposition, hegemony, and articulation—the analysis reveals how these responses construct meaning through rhetorical strategies rather than merely stating agreement or disagreement. The majority of psychiatric and medical organizations rejected the reports, employing consistent discursive patterns to defend existing paradigms, while only the British Psychological Society/Mental Health Europe joint response and the Canadian Minister of Health explicitly endorsed the Rapporteur's position. The study demonstrates how professional resistance to reform operates through deeply embedded discursive constructions of authority, scientific legitimacy, and progressive narratives. Key Entities World Psychiatric Association (WPA) (org) — One of the most frequent respondents; represented psychiatric authority in multiple responses World Medical Association (WMA) (org) — Frequently co-responded with WPA; defended

title: International research into 22 years of use of chemical restraint: An evidence overview
text: International research into 22 years of use of chemical restraint: An evidence overview Summary This systematic review analyzed 311 primary studies published between 1996 and 2018 on the use of chemical restraint (CR)—defined as the non-consenting administration of medication to manage acute aggression, agitation, or violence in adults with mental health conditions. The review employed PRISMA guidelines and was registered in PROSPERO (CRD 42017055258), searching PsycINFO, CINAHL, MEDLINE/PubMed, and Google Scholar for English-language peer-reviewed research. Studies were excluded if they involved minors, dementia-related agitation, forensic settings, or consenting treatment. The majority of research originated from the USA (25.7%), UK (18.6%), and Australia (11.3%), with only 5.8% involving international collaboration. Most studies were conducted in acute psychiatric wards (23.3%), general psychiatric wards (21.6%), and emergency departments (19.1%). The dominant research theme was medication effectiveness and safety (30.2% of studies), followed by patient perspectives (12.2%) and ward policies (11.6%). Despite increasing methodological sophistication over time, core research questions remained largely unchanged, with over 60 distinct outcome measures indicating a lack of standardization. The average publication lag was 3.4 years, longest for qualitative studies. Key Entities PRISMA framework (tool) — guided the systematic review methodology for study selection, data extracti

title: Implementation of guidelines on prevention of coercion and violence (PreVCo) in psychiatry: a multicentre randomised controlled trial
text: Implementation of guidelines on prevention of coercion and violence (PreVCo) in psychiatry: a multicentre randomised controlled trial Summary This study evaluated the effectiveness of implementing guideline-based recommendations to reduce coercive measures in psychiatric wards across Germany. Fifty-four psychiatric wards were randomized to either an intervention group receiving support from external implementation consultants to adopt three of twelve guideline recommendations, or a waitlist control group. The primary outcome was the number of coercive measures (seclusion, restraint, forced medication) per occupied bed per month during the final three months of the intervention period. Secondary outcomes included duration of coercive measures and assaults. Intervention fidelity was measured using the newly developed PreVCo Rating Tool, which assesses adherence to the selected recommendations on a scale from 0 to 135. After a three-month baseline period, the intervention lasted twelve months, with outcome data collected during the final three months to account for implementation latency. The intervention successfully improved guideline adherence, with intervention wards showing a significant increase in PreVCo Rating Tool scores (mean difference: 12.4 points, p < 0.001, Cohen’s d = 1.48). However, no statistically significant differences were found between groups in the primary or secondary outcomes related to coercive measures or assaults. Both groups showed reductions over ti

title: Ethical Issues in Clinical Decision-Making about Involuntary Psychiatric Treatment: A Scoping Review
text: Ethical Issues in Clinical Decision-Making about Involuntary Psychiatric Treatment: A Scoping Review Summary This scoping review maps and systematizes the literature on ethical issues in clinical decision-making about involuntary psychiatric treatment. The study analyzed 35 peer-reviewed articles from 14 countries (published between 2008 and 2023) identified through searches of Embase, PsycINFO, CINAHL, Medline, and Scopus. Using inductive content analysis, the review organized findings into five main categories: ethical foundations guiding decisions, criteria for involuntary treatment, gaps/barriers/risks, strategies to improve practice, and evidence-based recommendations. The review highlights persistent tensions between autonomy and beneficence, inconsistent application of legal criteria, and the prevalence of moral uncertainty among clinicians. It emphasizes the need for context-sensitive, participatory approaches grounded in human rights frameworks like the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD). title: Using coercion in mental disorders or risking the patients death An analysis of the protocols of a clinical ethics committee
text: Using coercion in mental disorders or risking the patients death An analysis of the protocols of a clinical ethics committee Summary This study analyzed 17 protocols from a German psychiatric hospital ethics committee spanning 2016 to 2023 to understand how ethical decisions about coercion are made in clinical practice. The majority of cases (13/17) involved determining whether coercion should be used in patients lacking decision-making capacity, primarily due to risk of death from suicide or starvation. Recommendations were evenly split between endorsing and refusing coercion, with consensus reached in most cases. A key innovation was the recommendation of self-binding advance directives (Ulysses contracts) in two cases involving fluctuating capacity, allowing time-limited coercive interventions during periods of incapacity. The analysis yielded a decision algorithm integrating capacity assessment, benefit-harm weighing, residual autonomy considerations, proportionality, and biopsychosocial context to guide ethically defensible coercion decisions. Key Entities Centers for Psychiatry Suedwuerttemberg (org): Operated four psychiatric hospitals providing the clinical data for the ethics committee analysis. Ulm University (org): Academic affiliation of the author, providing institutional context for the research. title: International Human Rights and Institutional Forensic Psychiatry: The Core Issues
text: International Human Rights and Institutional Forensic Psychiatry: The Core Issues Summary This chapter examines the systemic neglect of forensic psychiatric patients—those involved with the criminal justice system—in human rights discourse and protections, despite their exposure to conditions that routinely violate international law. The author argues that while civil psychiatric patients benefited from post-1970s "civil rights reforms" that improved conditions and enabled advocacy, forensic patients remained largely invisible, with minimal scholarly or advocacy attention to their plight. The ratification of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) and the Convention Against Torture (CAT) presents a pivotal opportunity to address this gap, as these treaties mandate humane treatment, prohibit torture, and require state accountability—yet forensic populations are seldom considered in their application. The author identifies six interconnected factors perpetuating this invisibility: (1) near-total absence of forensic patients in CRPD/CAT scholarship; (2) globally documented "shocking" abusive conditions (e.g., overcrowding, lack of basic services, sexual abuse in Argentina; inadequate care and transfer delays in England; misuse of untrained prisoners as monitors in Hungary; Albanian prison detention without reevaluation); (3) human rights bodies ignoring plaintiffs’ forensic status in landmark cases (e.g., Victor Rosario Congo where the Inter-American Com

title: Approval of Coercion in Psychiatry in Public Perception and the Role of Stigmatization
text: Approval of Coercion in Psychiatry in Public Perception and the Role of Stigmatization Summary This study examined public approval of coercive psychiatric measures through a vignette-based survey of 1,112 residents in Basel-Stadt, Switzerland. Participants evaluated scenarios involving individuals with psychiatric conditions (psychosis, borderline personality disorder, alcohol dependence) exhibiting varying levels of dangerous behavior. The researchers measured approval of three coercive interventions—involuntary hospitalization, medication, and seclusion—and assessed their relationship with stigma indicators (perceived dangerousness and desire for social distance), familiarity with mental illness, and perceived treatment usefulness. Results showed that approval was highest when individuals were seen as dangerous, when treatment was viewed as beneficial, and when actual dangerous behavior was present. Perceived dangerousness consistently predicted approval across all coercive types, while familiarity with mental illness reduced approval only for less severe measures (hospitalization), not for medication or seclusion. Desire for social distance predicted approval of medication and seclusion but not hospitalization. The study concludes that public attitudes align with legal frameworks but are significantly shaped by stigmatizing attitudes, especially for more invasive interventions. title: Human Rights and Psychiatric Coercion: The Legitimisation Problem for (Social) Psychiatry
text: Human Rights and Psychiatric Coercion: The Legitimisation Problem for (Social) Psychiatry Summary This chapter examines the tension between psychiatric practices involving coercion—such as involuntary hospitalization, restraint, and forced medication—and international human rights standards, particularly the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UN CRPD). It argues that the UN CRPD redefines legitimacy in psychiatric care by mandating that legal capacity cannot be denied based on mental health status, thereby challenging traditional justifications for coercion rooted in beneficence and paternalism. The author critiques both biomedical and social psychiatry for inconsistently applying autonomy principles, noting resistance even within progressive psychiatric communities to fully embracing supported decision-making. The analysis draws on UN committee interpretations, WHO guidelines, and scholarly debates to show how coercion in psychiatry is increasingly framed as a human rights violation akin to torture or degrading treatment. The chapter further extends this critique to Medical Aid in Dying (MAID), arguing that denying MAID to individuals with psychosocial problems constitutes discrimination under the UN CRPD, despite historical and ethical concerns within psychiatry. title: The Mysore Declaration
text: The Mysore Declaration Summary The Mysore Declaration emerged from a 2013 international symposium in Mysore, India, bringing together experts from the Indian Forensic Mental Health Association and the European Violence in Psychiatry Research Group to address the under-documented issue of coercion in Indian mental health care. The declaration defines coercive measures for the Indian context—including physical, chemical, mechanical, environmental restraint, seclusion, and psychological restraint—and outlines a multi-pronged strategy to reduce their use. This strategy includes raising awareness, developing evidence-based guidelines, implementing staff training, and establishing benchmarking systems using validated tools to monitor and compare practices. Central to the declaration is the affirmation that coercion, particularly when disproportionate or degrading, violates the human rights of persons with mental illness, while acknowledging the complex interplay between patient autonomy, potential benefits of involuntary treatment in restoring functioning, and the significant role of families in treatment decisions within Indian socio-cultural contexts. The declaration identifies legal ambiguities—such as the lack of competence definitions in the Indian Mental Health Act—as key structural barriers, alongside stigma, resource constraints, and inadequate advocacy, and calls for legal reform, institutional accountability, and increased awareness to align practice with human rights sta

title: The Role of the Psychiatrist in Seclusion and Restraint
text: The Role of the Psychiatrist in Seclusion and Restraint Summary This article examines the evolving role of psychiatrists in managing seclusion and restraint (S-R) in psychiatric inpatient and residential settings. Historically, psychiatrists have been involved in S-R oversight for over 200 years, but their specific responsibilities in prevention have remained undefined and inconsistent across facilities. The authors argue that psychiatrists should take a more active, leadership-oriented role in S-R reduction by engaging with the evidence-based Six Core Strategies to Prevent Conflict, Trauma, and Violence (6CS) model. This framework provides concrete opportunities for psychiatrists to lead organizational change, use data to guide practice, train staff, apply prevention tools, include patients and families in decision-making, and conduct therapeutic debriefings after S-R events. The article highlights successful implementations, such as the Pennsylvania State Hospital’s reduction in S-R hours and New York’s PARS program achieving 50–80% reductions over four years, as evidence that psychiatrist-led involvement within structured models like 6CS can improve safety and reduce harm. title: The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint: Prioritizing Patient Experience
text: The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint: Prioritizing Patient Experience Summary This commentary critiques the ethical framework proposed by Crutchfield and Redinger (2024) for chemical restraint use, arguing it inadequately addresses the psychological and social harms of psychotropic medications. The authors contend that chemical restraints can alter patient mental states in ways that produce non-authentic compliance, which clinicians may mistake for genuine informed consent. Drawing on patient testimonies and critical scholarship, they emphasize that ignoring lived experience risks epistemic injustice and overlooks systemic drivers such as sanism and cost-cutting in under-resourced healthcare systems. The piece advocates for ethical analysis centered on patient voice, capacity assessment as a prerequisite for consent, and restorative practices post-restraint to rebuild trust. title: Legal Aspects of the Use of Coercive Measures in Psychiatry
text: Legal Aspects of the Use of Coercive Measures in Psychiatry Summary This chapter examines how international and regional human rights frameworks constrain the use of coercive measures in psychiatric settings, with a focus on forensic psychiatry. It analyzes the legal standards governing involuntary commitment and treatment under the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) and the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UN CRPD), drawing on jurisprudence from the European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) and national courts like Germany’s Federal Constitutional Court. The author argues that while coercion remains present in psychiatric care, its application is increasingly governed by rights-based constraints requiring legal basis, necessity, proportionality, and procedural safeguards such as independent review and documentation. The chapter emphasizes that involuntary interventions must be justified not merely by medical necessity but by compliance with human rights standards, particularly Articles 5 (liberty) and 8 (privacy) of the ECHR, and Articles 12 (legal capacity) and 17 (integrity) of the UN CRPD. It concludes that robust monitoring, record-keeping, and independent oversight are essential for accountability and the protection of fundamental rights in psychiatric institutions. title: Surveying the Geneva impasse: Coercive care and human rights
text: Surveying the Geneva impasse: Coercive care and human rights Summary This paper examines the "Geneva Impasse," a disagreement within the United Nations human rights system regarding whether coercive care—such as involuntary psychiatric treatment—can ever comply with international human rights standards. The authors analyze two key UN documents: General Comment 35 (GC35) from the Human Rights Committee, which permits coercive care if it is necessary, proportionate, a last resort, and accompanied by safeguards; and the Guidelines on Article 14 (G14) and General Comment 1 (GC1) from the Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD Committee), which take an abolitionist stance by asserting that CRPD Article 14 permits no exceptions for deprivation of liberty based on disability. The paper distinguishes between non-consensual treatment (treatment without valid consent) and coercive treatment (treatment contrary to the patient’s reasonably ascertainable will or preference), and introduces a typology of abolitionist positions (A1, A2, A3) based on varying conditions under which intervention without consent is prohibited. The authors argue that the core of the disagreement lies in whether CRPD Article 14 expresses an absolute right—specifically, a right not to be deprived of liberty on the basis of disability—which would preclude any proportionality balancing. They conclude by proposing three avenues for moving beyond the impasse, though these are not detailed in the ab

title: Variations in definitions used for describing restrictive care practices (seclusion and restraint) in adult mental health inpatient units: a systematic review
text: Variations in definitions used for describing restrictive care practices (seclusion and restraint) in adult mental health inpatient units: a systematic review Summary This systematic review examined how restrictive care practices (RCPs)—physical/mechanical restraint, seclusion, and chemical restraint—are defined in recent literature from adult mental health inpatient units. The authors searched six databases (Scopus, MEDLINE, PsycINFO, Web of Science, Embase, CINAHL-Plus) for studies published between 2010 and 2023 that included explicit or implicit definitions of these practices. A total of 95 studies were included, with 72 defining physical/mechanical restraint, 65 defining seclusion, and 19 defining chemical restraint. Through inductive content analysis, the review identified six thematic elements that varied across definitions: methods used, reasons or desired outcomes, extent of patient restriction, timing (duration, frequency, time of day), level of patient autonomy, and personnel involved. No universally accepted definition was found for any RCP type. For example, physical restraint ranged from broad definitions like "any physical/manual or mechanical methods" to narrow ones like "holding the person’s arm." Seclusion definitions varied from locked rooms to non-locked isolation with staff supervision. Chemical restraint was least frequently defined and often conflated with treatment (e.g., antipsychotics for agitation). These inconsistencies hinder valid comparisons of

title: Human Rights in Psychiatry
text: Human Rights in Psychiatry Summary This chapter analyzes the evolution of mental health care through three historical phases: Phase 1 (until ~1970s) disregarded human rights with institution-centered care; Phase 2 (1980s-present) acknowledges human rights but permits restriction for medical reasons through person-centered, evidence-based approaches; and proposes Phase 3 where human rights form the basis of support through person-driven, preference-based care. The core argument is that psychosocial support should prioritize service user preferences, with clinical evidence serving only when aligned with those preferences, based on the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) Article 12 on legal capacity. The chapter details how supported decision-making—not shared decision-making—is required by human rights frameworks, necessitating multi-professional teams without professional dominance and peer involvement in leadership. Implementation requires eliminating all coercion (formal, informal, administrative) except self-binding advance directives (Ulysses clauses), basing care settings and treatment modalities on user preference, focusing on individual life goals rather than symptom management, and involving service users as leaders in research and training rather than subjects. title: Is Involuntary Placement and Non-Consensual Treatment Ever Compliant with UN Human Rights Standards?
text: Is Involuntary Placement and Non-Consensual Treatment Ever Compliant with UN Human Rights Standards? Summary This report surveys United Nations statements from 2006 to 2017 to examine whether involuntary placement (detention without consent) and non-consensual treatment (medical intervention without consent) can comply with international human rights standards. Prepared by the Essex Autonomy Project, it analyzes positions from UN human rights mechanisms including treaty-based bodies (such as the Human Rights Committee and CRPD Committee), charter-based bodies (Human Rights Council, Universal Periodic Review), and special procedures (Special Rapporteurs). The study finds no unified UN position, revealing significant divergence: some bodies like the OHCHR and CRPD Committee take an abolitionist stance, arguing any deprivation of liberty based on disability is discriminatory and unlawful, while others like the Human Rights Committee, CAT Committee, and STP permit such measures only under strict conditions—necessity, proportionality, last resort, and with procedural safeguards. The report highlights evolving interpretations, particularly the shift in the UN Special Rapporteur on torture from conditional allowance to advocating an absolute ban on non-consensual measures. title: Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients: Theoretical and Empirical Evidence
text: Treatment Options for Acute Agitation in Psychiatric Patients: Theoretical and Empirical Evidence Summary This review examines pharmacological treatments for acute agitation in psychiatric patients, focusing on the concept of rapid tranquilization—the use of drugs to rapidly calm agitated or aggressive individuals in emergency or psychiatric settings. The authors analyze a range of agents including typical antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol), benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam, diazepam), and atypical antipsychotics (e.g., olanzapine, ziprasidone, aripiprazole), evaluating their pharmacological profiles, efficacy, safety, and supporting clinical evidence. The review emphasizes that treatment selection should be individualized based on patient presentation, desired clinical effect (sedation vs. non-sedating calm), drug availability, and side effect profile. It synthesizes data from clinical trials and systematic reviews to assess the quality of evidence for each option, noting significant limitations in the literature such as heterogeneity, small sample sizes, and lack of standardization. The authors conclude that haloperidol, either alone or in combination with anticholinergics like promethazine, remains a strong candidate due to its extensive evidence base (41 RCTs, n=4933), accessibility, and efficacy in controlling agitation without necessarily inducing deep sedation. The combination of haloperidol and promethazine is highlighted as superior to haloperidol alone, offering red

title: Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders: a systematic review
text: Machine Learning for prediction of violent behaviors in schizophrenia spectrum disorders: a systematic review Summary This systematic review evaluates the application of machine learning (ML) techniques to predict violent behavior (VB) in individuals diagnosed with schizophrenia spectrum disorders (SSD). The analysis synthesized 18 studies comprising 11,733 patients, assessing various ML algorithms including support vector machines, random forests, and gradient boosting models. Outcomes were measured using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and accuracy, with VB defined heterogeneously across studies—ranging from verbal aggression to homicide. Gradient boosting consistently demonstrated superior predictive performance compared to other algorithms, achieving AUROC values up to 0.95 in individual studies. Key predictors identified across models included age, antipsychotic dosage (particularly olanzapine equivalent), psychiatric symptom severity (e.g., PANSS scores), and socioenvironmental factors such as prior hospitalizations and social isolation. The review highlights the potential of ML to augment clinical risk assessment but emphasizes challenges related to outcome heterogeneity, limited external validation, and ethical concerns regarding predictive labeling in psychiatric care. title: Is Coercive Treatment Necessary or a Method of Mass Control?
text: Is Coercive Treatment Necessary or a Method of Mass Control? Summary This source critically examines coercive treatment—defined as forced psychiatric or medical intervention without consent—through historical, legal, and sociological lenses. It traces the origins of modern coercive practices to 19th-century UK psychiatric legislation, including the Lunatic Asylums Act of 1845 and associated commissions, which granted psychiatric professionals authority over individuals deemed of "unsound mind." The analysis argues that while coercive treatment may be justified in cases of imminent danger (e.g., under California’s Welfare and Institutions Code Section 5150), its application has been systematically skewed against Black, Indigenous, and other marginalized populations. Drawing on scholarship from Angela Davis, Michelle Alexander, and Joy DeGruy, the author traces a direct lineage from slavery-era mechanisms like Black Codes and slave patrols to modern systems of mass incarceration and coercive psychiatric commitment, framing these as interconnected tools of racialized social control. The source further incorporates epigenetic research to argue that trauma from these systems is biologically transmitted across generations, exacerbating harm in vulnerable communities. Ultimately, it concludes that coercive treatment often serves not to treat illness but to enforce conformity to dominant power structures, particularly white supremacy. Key Entities Cardiff University (org): Cited for

title: The UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities 'Rights, will and preferences' in relation to mental health disabilities
text: The UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities 'Rights, will and preferences' in relation to mental health disabilities Summary This paper analyzes the meaning of "rights, will and preferences" under Article 12 of the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), particularly in mental health contexts where a person's current expressed preference may conflict with their prior advance directive or enduring values. Drawing on philosophical concepts of will (Kant, Bratman), legal precedents from the German Federal Constitutional Court and English Court of Protection, and interpretive frameworks like Davidson's radical interpretation, the author argues that "will" represents a higher-order reflection of deeply held values and commitments, distinct from transient preferences. When these diverge—such as during acute mental health episodes—respecting the person's will (e.g., by following an advance directive) may justify overriding current preferences without constituting impermissible substitute decision-making under the CRPD. The paper concludes that legal capacity must be upheld, and decisions should aim to facilitate the expression of enduring will, supported by interpretive efforts and safeguards, rather than defaulting to best interests or blanket prohibitions on intervention. Key Entities United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) (org): The international treaty establishing rights for persons with disabilities

title: The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care: A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention
text: The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care: A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention Summary This article examines how U.S. mental health law creates exceptions to bodily autonomy rights for people with psychosocial disabilities, permitting involuntary psychiatric treatment while simultaneously restricting access to voluntary, culturally responsive care. Through historical, legal, and human rights analysis, Bergan demonstrates that psychiatric labels have been historically used to pathologize marginalized groups—including Black, Indigenous, poor, queer, and disabled communities—and that civil commitment laws function as tools of social control rather than genuine medical intervention. The article contrasts the strong constitutional protections for refusing somatic medical treatment (e.g., in Cruzan v. Director) with the lack of equivalent protections in psychiatric contexts, arguing that this disparity stems from epistemic injustice and sanism. Drawing on international human rights frameworks like the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) and the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR), the author contends that forced psychiatric treatment violates rights to liberty, bodily integrity, and health. The article concludes that ensuring parity between the right to refuse somatic and psychiatric care, expanding access to community-based alternatives, and centering the lived experienc

title: A wicked problem: Chemical restraint: towards a definition
text: A wicked problem: Chemical restraint: towards a definition Summary This editorial examines the conceptual confusion surrounding the term "chemical restraint" in psychiatric care, arguing that inconsistent terminology impedes clear communication, research, and policy. The author reviews how terms like rapid tranquilization, urgent sedation, and therapeutic sedation are used interchangeably with chemical restraint across literature and guidelines, creating ambiguity about when and how psychotropic medications should be used to manage acute agitation or violence. Drawing on prior systematic reviews and clinical guidelines (notably NICE 2015), the piece emphasizes that chemical restraint should be understood as a last-resort, emergency intervention aimed at safety and facilitating psychiatric evaluation, while advocating for standardized terminology to improve clinical practice and reduce harm. title: Comparing mental health and mental capacity law data across borders; Challenges and opportunities
text: Comparing mental health and mental capacity law data across borders; Challenges and opportunities Summary This article examines the opportunities and challenges for comparing mental health and mental health capacity law between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland. It notes that despite similar geographical, demographic, and publicly funded health structures, meaningful cross-jurisdictional comparison is hindered by inconsistent data collection practices. The authors find that current data focuses on process metrics like numbers of assessments and involuntary admissions, with limited demographic analysis and minimal capture of service user or carer experiences. Drawing on international examples from Australia, Canada, and European nations, they argue that effective outcomes frameworks must include social determinants of health, holistic wellbeing indicators, and perspectives from service users and carers. The article concludes that a coordinated, inclusive approach to data harmonization—grounded in co-production—is essential to enable policy learning, improve services, and uphold human rights under frameworks like the UNCRPD. title: Ethical issues of participating in psychiatric research on coercion
text: Ethical issues of participating in psychiatric research on coercion Summary This chapter examines the ethical challenges of conducting psychiatric research involving individuals subjected to coercion, emphasizing that while such research is essential for improving understanding and treatment, it raises unique concerns about informed consent, decisional capacity, and voluntarism. The author analyzes international ethical frameworks—including the Nuremberg Code, Declaration of Helsinki, CIOMS guidelines, and WPA declarations—and applies them to real-world examples like the multinational EUNOMIA study and the Nordic study on coercion. Key issues include the difficulty of assessing capacity in acutely ill patients, the risk of therapeutic misconception, and the ethical problems with proxy consent when individuals later regain capacity. Despite these challenges, the chapter argues that ethically sound research is possible through rigorous processes such as independent information provision, repeated consent checks, training interviewers, and prioritizing assent when capacity is regained. Studies like EUNOMIA and the Nordic project demonstrated that careful procedures can minimize risk, with most participants reporting voluntary participation, though cultural and procedural variations across sites highlight the need for contextual sensitivity. title: Mental Health Workers’ Experiences of Using Coercive Measures: “You can’t tell people who don’t understand”
text: Mental Health Workers’ Experiences of Using Coercive Measures: “You can’t tell people who don’t understand” Summary This study explores the experiences of healthcare workers using coercive measures in a high-security psychiatric hospital in England, specifically Rampton Hospital. Through narrative interviews with 28 staff members including nurses, healthcare assistants, managers, and clinicians, the research investigates how staff navigate the tension between their therapeutic roles and security obligations. The findings reveal that decisions to use coercive measures are driven by accountability to both NHS and legal authorities (Ministry of Justice/Home Office), safety concerns for patients, staff, and the public, and the perception of having no alternative in dangerous situations. Staff frequently describe coercive measures as a "necessary evil" while emphasizing that patients are not prisoners, yet they develop emotional detachment and desensitization over time as coping strategies to manage the psychological burden of these procedures, often through rituals, routinization, and emotional distancing from patients' backgrounds. Key Entities Rampton Hospital (org): High-security psychiatric hospital in Nottinghamshire, England, where the study was conducted; the primary site of data collection involving 28 staff interviews across multiple wards. NHS (org): National Health Service employing the healthcare workers; represents the healthcare and therapeutic obligations of staff

title: Mandated Psychiatric Treatment in the Community–Forms, Prevalence, Outcomes and Controversies
text: Mandated Psychiatric Treatment in the Community–Forms, Prevalence, Outcomes and Controversies Summary This chapter reframes outpatient commitment as one form of "mandated community treatment" where leverage—applied through housing, financial benefits, or criminal justice systems—encourages treatment adherence in the community. Drawing on a five-site U.S. study of over 1,000 outpatients, it finds that 51% experienced at least one form of leverage in their lifetime, with housing avoidance (32%) and jail avoidance (23%) being most common, while traditional outpatient commitment (to avoid hospitalization) was least prevalent (15%). The chapter synthesizes preliminary findings from the MacArthur Research Network on mandated community treatment, showing that mental health courts reduce recidivism and are preferred by defendants, housing-as-leverage increases perceived coercion but Housing-First models yield comparable satisfaction, and psychiatric advance directives—when facilitated—reduce coercive crisis interventions. It concludes that leverage mechanisms vary in ethical acceptability and effectiveness depending on whether benefits are framed as rights or discretionary privileges, and critiques the overuse of "coercion" as a concept, proposing instead a contract framework to better capture mutual obligation in treatment agreements. Key Entities Treatment Advocacy Center (org): Advocacy group promoting assisted outpatient treatment; cited for positive outcome interpretations in Ta

title: An International Perspective on the Use of Coercive Measures
text: An International Perspective on the Use of Coercive Measures Summary This chapter examines the international use of coercive measures in psychiatric care, identifying four core types: therapy by coercion (e.g., antipsychotics), therapeutic measures without primary therapeutic intent (e.g., chemical restraint), separation (e.g., seclusion), and mechanical restriction (e.g., restraints). It argues that practices are shaped more by tradition and local norms than evidence, with significant variation in what is considered acceptable—such as seclusion being banned in Denmark but preferred in the Netherlands, or mechanical restraint prohibited in the UK but used elsewhere. Drawing on epidemiological data from studies like EUNOMIA and national registries, the chapter shows that coercion affects 3–16% of psychiatric admissions internationally, with wide differences in duration and frequency. Crucially, it finds that banning one coercive method often leads to increased use of others (substitution effect), and that the least restrictive option depends on individual patient preferences and context rather than a fixed hierarchy. Patient-reported data using tools like the Coercion Experience Scale (CES) reveal significant subjective distress and dignity violations, particularly with methods perceived as dehumanizing (e.g., net beds), challenging purely safety- or efficacy-based evaluations and emphasizing the need for ethically grounded, patient-centered approaches. title: Exploring psychiatric patient restraints: Balancing safety, ethics, and patient rights in mental healthcare
text: Exploring psychiatric patient restraints: Balancing safety, ethics, and patient rights in mental healthcare Summary This paper examines the complex ethical and clinical challenge of using restraints in psychiatric settings, balancing the need for patient and staff safety against the risks of harm and violations of patient rights. It synthesizes epidemiological, clinical, legal, and ethical literature to analyze the prevalence, types, consequences, and regulatory frameworks surrounding restraint use, with a particular focus on India’s Mental Healthcare Act of 2017. The authors highlight that restraints—physical, chemical, psychological, and seclusion—are frequently employed in response to aggression or treatment resistance but can lead to serious adverse outcomes such as positional asphyxia, thrombosis, rhabdomyolysis, and psychological trauma. The paper emphasizes that restraint should be a last resort, guided by de-escalation techniques, proper monitoring, documentation, and adherence to legal standards that uphold dignity, autonomy, and therapeutic alliance. Key Entities Mental Healthcare Act of 2017 (India) (type: org) — Serves as the central legislative framework discussed in the paper, establishing guidelines for restraint use, mandating documentation, monitoring, and protections for patient rights in Indian mental health facilities. title: Abolition of coercion in mental health services – A European survey of feasibility
text: Abolition of coercion in mental health services – A European survey of feasibility Summary This study surveyed members of the FOSTREN (Fostering and Strengthening Approaches to Reducing Coercion in European Mental Health Services) network to assess the perceived feasibility of totally abolishing psychiatric coercion in European mental health services. Using a mixed-methods web-based survey distributed to 167 network members, 76 respondents (45.5% response rate) answered whether complete abolition is achievable and explained their reasoning. Quantitative analysis showed only 31% (22 respondents) believed abolition is achievable, while 69% (49 respondents) did not. Qualitative thematic analysis revealed that skeptics cited the difficulty of treating severe mental illness, risks of violence, and the need to protect individuals from harm as key barriers, while proponents argued that the harms of coercion (trauma, death, injury) outweigh any benefits and that psychiatric advance directives and better training in alternatives like de-escalation could suffice. Younger age (<51 years) and less work experience (<16 years) were significantly associated with belief in achievability. Respondents also expressed greater optimism about eliminating in-patient coercion versus involuntary commitment and ranked mechanical restraint as the top priority for abolition if full abolition is unattainable. title: Physical and Pharmacological Restraints in Hospital Care: Protocol for a Systematic Review
text: Physical and Pharmacological Restraints in Hospital Care: Protocol for a Systematic Review Summary This document presents a protocol for a systematic review investigating the beneficial and adverse effects of physical and pharmacological restraints in hospital settings. Physical restraints are defined as measures limiting freedom of movement (e.g., bed rails, abdominal restraints), while pharmacological restraints involve drugs used to control behavior without treating a medical condition (e.g., benzodiazepines, antipsychotics for sedation). The authors note increasing concern about restraint use in hospitals despite a lack of consensus on guidelines and limited evidence from non-hospital settings (e.g., nursing homes) that may not generalize. The review aims to fill this gap by synthesizing evidence on outcomes such as length of hospital stay, complication rates, and behavioral measures to inform clinical practice and guideline development. The methodology follows PRISMA-P guidelines, with searches in MEDLINE, Embase, and PsycINFO, supplemented by reference scanning and VOS analysis for search refinement. Eligible studies include observational designs and, if available, RCTs conducted in general hospital wards (excluding ICU and psychiatric settings). Data will be extracted on patient characteristics, interventions, comparators, and predefined outcomes. Risk of bias will be assessed using the Cochrane Collaboration Tool (for RCTs) and Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (for observationa

title: The Psychiatrist's Double Bind; The Right to Refuse Medication
text: The Psychiatrist's Double Bind; The Right to Refuse Medication Summary The article analyzes the ethical and legal dilemma psychiatrists face when patients refuse medication, framed as a "double bind" between clinical beneficence and constitutional rights. Based on the landmark Rogers v Boston State Hospital case (1979), it establishes that patients possess a constitutional right to refuse medication grounded in privacy, liberty, and freedom of expression, except in emergencies involving substantial likelihood of extreme violence, personal injury, or attempted suicide. The article critiques guardianship as an impractical solution due to systemic barriers like patient incomprehension, guardian unavailability, cost, and delays that undermine therapeutic alliance. It details the Massachusetts Mental Health Center's protocol for critical (non-emergency) situations requiring dual psychiatrist confirmation and interim guardianship, while highlighting tensions between procedural ideals and clinical realities in understaffed public hospitals. title: Wearable Biosensing to Predict Imminent Aggressive Behavior in Psychiatric Inpatient Youths With Autism
text: Wearable Biosensing to Predict Imminent Aggressive Behavior in Psychiatric Inpatient Youths With Autism Summary This study investigated whether machine learning analysis of peripheral physiological signals from wearable biosensors could predict imminent aggressive behavior in psychiatrically hospitalized youth with autism. Researchers analyzed data from 70 participants across four U.S. psychiatric inpatient hospitals, recording 429 observation sessions totaling 497 hours and documenting 6,665 episodes of self-injurious behavior (SIB), emotion dysregulation (ED), and aggression toward others (ATO). Using the Empatica E4 wristband to capture cardiovascular activity, electrodermal activity, and motion data, the researchers tested multiple machine learning classifiers including logistic regression (LR), support vector machines (SVM), and neural networks (NN). The study found that LR emerged as the most consistent performer, achieving a mean area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.80 when predicting aggressive behavior three minutes before onset. Feature engineering with augmented feature vectors (AFVs) that incorporated temporal context improved prediction, and domain adaptation techniques enhanced individual model personalization. The findings suggest wearable biosensing could enable preemptive intervention strategies for managing aggression in this population. title: Ethics and medical practice: Why psychiatry is unique
text: Ethics and medical practice: Why psychiatry is unique Summary This article examines the distinctive ethical challenges in psychiatric practice compared to general medicine. It argues that psychiatry requires heightened ethical standards due to the unique nature of mental illness, patient vulnerability, societal stigma, and the deeply personal disclosures inherent in therapeutic relationships. The authors analyze core ethical domains—confidentiality, boundary management, individual and cultural sensitivity, and professionalism—highlighting how psychiatric contexts create distinct dilemmas. They emphasize that while general medical ethics principles apply, psychiatry demands special attention to legally recognized limits on confidentiality (such as duty to warn and parens patriae), nuanced boundary management distinguishing harmful violations from potentially beneficial crossings, greater reliance on narrative ethics alongside principlism to understand patient context, and vigilant professionalism to prevent misuse of psychiatric expertise (e.g., in interrogation settings). The paper supports its claims with references to landmark legal cases (Tarasoff), federal regulations (HIPAA), professional guidelines (APA, AMA), and established ethics literature, illustrating real-world dilemmas through clinical examples like marital discord, sexual dysfunction, and employer inquiries.
