title: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights - Chapter 4: Article 12 and Autonomy
text: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights - Chapter 4: Article 12 and Autonomy Summary This chapter critically examines competing conceptions of autonomy in the context of CRPD Article 12, which guarantees equal recognition before the law for persons with disabilities. It begins by outlining the traditional liberal view of autonomy rooted in Kantian rationality and Millian non-interference, which excludes people with cognitive disability by equating autonomy with rational independence. The chapter then evaluates two broad strategies to make autonomy inclusive: first, redefining autonomy within liberalism by divorcing it from rationality (e.g., emphasizing emotions, narrative coherence, or relational autonomy); and second, decoupling autonomy from personhood altogether to allow substituted decision-making as a fallback. It critiques the first strategy for ignoring the residual impact of cognitive impairment and risking surrogate overreach, while finding the second insufficiently protective of autonomy. Ultimately, the chapter proposes that autonomy as an achievement—requiring both decision-making competencies and real options enabled by support—best aligns with Article 12. This achievement model acknowledges that autonomy may require state intervention, involve costs, be progressively realizable, and thus embody the indivisibility of human rights by blending civil-political (legal capacity) and socio-economic (support) dimensions. title: Advance Directives: A Tool for Reducing Coercion
text: Advance Directives: A Tool for Reducing Coercion Summary This 2012 article by Richard J. Bonnie proposes psychiatric advance directives (PADs) as a strategic tool to reduce coercion in mental health care by enabling individuals to document treatment preferences in advance of decision-making incapacity. The author argues that widespread use of advance directives can shift psychiatric care from a model of coercion to one of contractual agreement, particularly when integrated into routine care and supported by systemic initiatives. The article highlights Virginia’s statewide effort to promote advance directive completion through collaboration among hospitals, community agencies, legal professionals, and consumer advocacy groups, supported by a dedicated informational website. Bonnie contends that as advance directive use becomes normalized, coercive interventions will be needed less frequently and, when used, will align with prior consent, reducing their perceived coerciveness. He further suggests that the proportion of individuals with executed advance directives could serve as a measurable indicator of progress toward a recovery-oriented mental health system. Key Entities Virginia (type: org) — Central focus of the case study; implemented a statewide initiative to promote advance directives through multi-stakeholder collaboration. title: Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis
text: Patients’ and staff members’ experiences of restrictive practices in acute mental health in-patient settings; systematic review and thematic synthesis Summary This source presents a systematic review and thematic synthesis of qualitative research examining the experiences of restrictive practices (such as physical restraint, chemical restraint, and seclusion) in acute mental health inpatient settings from both patient and staff perspectives. The study analyzed 21 qualitative papers published between 1996 and 2019 across 11 countries, using a pre-registered protocol (PROSPERO: CRD42020176859) and following PRISMA guidelines. Study quality was assessed using the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (CASP) tool, and data were synthesized using thematic synthesis via QSR’s NVivo 12 software. Four overarching themes emerged from patient data: psychological effects, staff communication, loss of human rights, and making changes. From staff data, four themes emerged: the need for restrictive practices, psychological impact, decision-making, and making changes. The findings indicate that restrictive practices are experienced as overwhelmingly negative by both groups, causing psychological harm such as fear, anxiety, and re-traumatization, damaging staff–patient relationships, and persisting beyond the event, even when staff view them as a necessary last resort. Key Entities PROSPERO (org) — International prospective register of systematic reviews where the review protocol was pre-regis

title: Treatment without consent in adult psychiatry inpatient units: a retrospective study on predictive factors
text: Treatment without consent in adult psychiatry inpatient units: a retrospective study on predictive factors Summary This study investigates predictors of non-emergency forced medication under Swiss Civil Code Article 434 in adult psychiatry inpatient units at Geneva University Hospitals between 2018 and 2021. Researchers conducted a retrospective analysis of 4,326 patients, identifying 71 individuals (1.64%) who received repeated medication without consent for psychiatric treatment outside emergency situations. The study examined demographic and clinical variables including age, gender, admission status, primary diagnosis, and symptom burden measured by the Health of the Nation Outcome Scales (HoNOS). Univariate and multivariate analyses revealed that female gender, psychotic disorder diagnosis, and involuntary admission were significant predictors of Article 434 medication, while neither global HoNOS scores nor aggression-specific items were predictive. The primary justification for forced medication was risk of self-endangerment due to psychosocial deterioration (53.5% of cases), challenging assumptions that aggression drives most psychiatric coercion. Key Entities Geneva University Hospitals (org): The healthcare institution where the study was conducted, providing the clinical setting and patient data for the retrospective analysis. title: Ward Design, Coercion and Aggression in Psychiatric (Intensive) Care
text: Ward Design, Coercion and Aggression in Psychiatric (Intensive) Care Summary This chapter examines the relationship between psychiatric ward design and the occurrence of aggression, seclusion, and coercive measures, focusing on Psychiatric Intensive Care Units (PICUs). It traces the historical evolution of PICUs from staff-centered models to designs incorporating healing environment principles, supported by evidence from multiple countries. Key evidence includes a large Dutch observational study linking design features such as limited outdoor space and high patient density to increased seclusion, while private space and comfort reduced it. The chapter also presents a case study of the Amsterdam UMC ward renovation, which replaced closed nursing stations with an open front desk, created distinct living and working zones, and used natural materials—resulting in improved safety perceptions and reduced coercion despite initial staff skepticism. Theoretical grounding is provided by Ulrich et al.'s (2018) stress-reduction model, which posits that environmental stressors increase negative emotions and aggression, while design elements like natural light, nature views, and perceived control mitigate these effects. The authors conclude that ward design is a modifiable risk factor for aggression and coercion, and that integrating healing environment principles—such as single rooms, access to nature, privacy, and therapeutic spaces—can improve outcomes in psychiatric intensive care. title: Who’s the Boss? Ethical Dilemmas in the Treatment of Children and Adolescents
text: Who’s the Boss? Ethical Dilemmas in the Treatment of Children and Adolescents Summary This article examines ethical challenges in psychiatric care for children and adolescents through three clinical vignettes. It explores tensions between adolescent autonomy (via assent), parental authority, confidentiality, and legal obligations such as mandatory reporting. The authors analyze scenarios involving substance use disclosure, adolescent romantic relationships, and exposure to intimate partner violence, emphasizing the need for context-sensitive judgment grounded in professional ethics codes (e.g., AACAP), legal frameworks like HIPAA, and evolving societal norms. The piece highlights how emerging issues—such as teen sexting and diverse family structures—complicate traditional ethical boundaries, requiring clinicians to navigate conflicts between therapeutic trust and legal compliance. Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA) (type: tool) — Federal law governing privacy of protected health information, applied to both minor and adult patients in psychiatric care. title: The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care
text: The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care Summary This chapter synthesizes research on patient experiences of coercive measures in psychiatric care, particularly focusing on seclusion and restraint in forensic and general psychiatric settings. Drawing on studies from the 1970s onward, it examines how patients perceive interventions such as forced medication, seclusion, and restraint, highlighting that while these are often viewed as unnecessary or punitive, some patients report positive experiences like safety, privacy, or emotional relief—especially when accompanied by clear communication, respect, and therapeutic relationships. The authors emphasize that perceptions vary by factors including age, gender, trauma history, and clinical context (e.g., forensic vs. general psychiatric wards), and stress that patient involvement in care planning and staff training in de-escalation and trauma-informed care are critical to reducing reliance on coercion. Although certain practices may coincidentally correlate with improved pre-discharge quality of life (possibly due to contrast with impending freedom), the authors caution against interpreting this as therapeutic benefit and advocate for prevention through human-centered care rather than reliance on coercive interventions. title: Informed Consent and the Right to Refuse Treatment
text: Informed Consent and the Right to Refuse Treatment Summary This chapter examines the legal and clinical foundations of informed consent and the right to refuse treatment in psychiatric care. It identifies three core components of valid consent: competency (the patient's ability to understand, appreciate, reason, and communicate a choice), adequate information disclosure (what a reasonable patient would want to know), and voluntariness (absence of coercion). Competency is determined situationally through functional assessment rather than psychiatric diagnosis, meaning a patient may understand information but lack appreciation due to depression or psychosis. The chapter details exceptions to consent, including emergencies (imminent serious harm preventing consent) and therapeutic privilege (withholding information only if disclosure would cause severe psychological harm, not merely to prevent treatment refusal). Regarding the right to refuse treatment, it explains that while institutionalized patients have a qualified right, this can be overridden in emergencies or when patients are deemed incompetent, requiring substituted judgment via guardians, advance directives, or court approval. The text emphasizes that consent is an ongoing process, not a one-time event, and warns against overreliance on written forms, advocating instead for thorough clinical documentation of the consent process in patient records. Key Entities American Psychiatric Publishing (organization: publisher) —

title: Coercion in Contemporary Mental Health Services: Key Concepts, Historical Development and Contextual Factors
text: Coercion in Contemporary Mental Health Services: Key Concepts, Historical Development and Contextual Factors Summary This chapter examines coercion in contemporary mental health services through three lenses: key concepts and terminology, historical development, and contextual factors influencing current practices. It distinguishes between formal coercion (legally sanctioned interventions like involuntary hospitalization, medication, mechanical restraint, and seclusion) and informal coercion (communicative pressure without explicit legal backing), emphasizing that formal coercion's reliance on legal processes should make monitoring feasible, yet significant variability in reported rates persists due to inconsistent recording systems rather than definitional ambiguities. The authors trace historical attitudes from pre-modern restraint and isolation through Enlightenment-era moral treatment reforms, post-WWII human rights developments (especially the CRPD), and deinstitutionalization movements, noting that while these reduced some overt abuses, coercion persists in new forms. Contemporary epidemiological data reveals extreme variability in coercion rates across studies (e.g., mechanical restraint from 0.3%-54%, seclusion 0.2%-56%, chemical restraint 2%-58%), with population-based metrics showing median rates of 29 restrictive practices per 100,000/year in the Netherlands versus 18 in the UK. title: The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint: Prioritizing Patient Experience
text: The Lived Realities of Chemical Restraint: Prioritizing Patient Experience Summary This commentary critiques prevailing ethical frameworks for chemical restraint, arguing they inadequately address the psychological and experiential harms caused by psychotropic drugs altering patient states. Drawing on patient testimonies and clinical ethnography, the authors contend that restored decisional capacity post-restraint does not equate to authentic self-determination, as drug-induced preferences or behavioral compliance may mask coercion. They emphasize systemic drivers like underfunded psychosocial care and bias against disabled populations, positioning chemical restraint as a last resort requiring post-intervention restorative efforts to rebuild trust and mitigate epistemic injustice. Key Entities AJOB Neuroscience (Journal): The peer-reviewed journal publishing this commentary, providing academic venue and credibility in neuroethics. Center for Medical Ethics and Health Policy, Baylor College of Medicine (Organization): Affiliation of all three authors, situating the work within a prominent bioethics research institution. Psychotropic drugs (Tool/Substance): The pharmacological agents (e.g., antipsychotics, benzodiazepines) used to achieve chemical restraint; central to the analysis of their mind-altering effects on patient autonomy and expression. title: Prevalence and variability in use of physical and chemical restraints in residential aged care facilities; A systematic review and meta-analysis
text: Prevalence and variability in use of physical and chemical restraints in residential aged care facilities; A systematic review and meta-analysis Summary This study conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis to estimate the prevalence and variability of physical and chemical restraint use in residential aged care facilities worldwide. A total of 85 studies were included, with 50 contributing to the analysis of physical restraint and 7 to chemical restraint. The pooled prevalence of any physical restraint was 33% (95% CI: 0.31–0.35) and any chemical restraint was 32% (95% CI: 0.23–0.42), both showing extreme heterogeneity (I² = 99.9%). Bedrails (44%) and benzodiazepines (42%) were the most common specific restraints. Subgroup analysis revealed significant regional variation, with the lowest prevalence in mixed regions (20%) and North America (22%), and the highest in Australia (65%). Measurement approach strongly influenced estimates, with direct observation (49%) and combined methods (42%) yielding higher prevalence than record review (28%), staff interview (27%), or administrative data (20%). Meta-regression indicated that North American studies (lower prevalence), direct observation (higher), and combined methods (higher) independently explained 25.5% of heterogeneity in physical restraint prevalence. For chemical restraint, only dementia-specific care and Asian region showed significant univariate associations, but multivariate modeling was not feasible due to limited

title: Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 03
text: Clinical Handbook of Psychiatry and Law - 03 Summary This chapter analyzes the legal doctrine of the right to treatment in psychiatric inpatient settings, tracing its evolution from early 20th-century origins through landmark cases like Wyatt v. Stickney and Youngberg v. Romeo. It establishes that the right applies only to involuntarily committed individuals and guarantees only minimal, constitutionally adequate care—not optimal, effective, or preferred treatment. The chapter details enforcement mechanisms such as consent decrees and federal legislation like CRIPA, while noting judicial limitations in compelling legislative appropriations. It further explores the related principle of least restrictive alternative (LRA), its theoretical roots in cases like Shelton v. Tucker, and its modern application via the ADA and Olmstead decision, highlighting persistent gaps between legal ideals and practical implementation due to insufficient community resources. The analysis concludes that despite historical advances in patient rights, systemic barriers—including underfunding, stigma, and the decline of long-term inpatient care—undermine the full realization of these doctrines in contemporary mental health systems. Key Entities U.S. Supreme Court (organization): Central to interpreting constitutional rights in psychiatry, particularly in Wyatt v. Stickney, Youngberg v. Romeo, and Olmstead decisions. Wyatt v. Stickney (case): Landmark 1971 case establishing a constitutional right to tre

title: Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias
text: Responding to the UN Special Rapporteur's anti-psychiatry bias Summary This article critiques the 2017 UN Special Rapporteur's report on the right to mental health, arguing that it displays an anti-psychiatry bias by disproportionately attributing human rights abuses to biomedical psychiatry and the misuse of medical evidence. The authors contend that the report overlooks critical structural barriers to mental health care, particularly in low- and middle-income countries, where treatment gaps exceed 75% due to underfunding and lack of access—not biomedical ideology. They further argue that the report mischaracterizes inpatient psychiatric care as inherently harmful and selectively cites antidepressant efficacy data, ignoring evidence that such treatments are effective for severe cases in resource-poor settings. While acknowledging valid concerns about corporate influence on biomedical research, the article maintains that the report's ideological stance undermines global mental health efforts by failing to balance biomedical and psychosocial approaches. The authors advocate for context-sensitive, evidence-based care that prioritizes access to any effective treatment, whether biological or psychosocial, to uphold the dignity and rights of individuals with mental illness. Key Entities Australian & New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry: Academic journal — Venue where the critique was published, providing a platform for psychiatric response to UN human rights discourse Royal Australia

title: Chemical restraint as behavioural euthanasia; case studies from the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety
text: Chemical restraint as behavioural euthanasia; case studies from the Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety Summary This study analyzed publicly available documents from the Australian Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety to identify precursors and mediating factors influencing the use of chemical restraint in residential aged care. Using a descriptive case study approach, researchers examined seven cases where older people were prescribed or administered psychotropic medication under circumstances suggesting chemical restraint. Four consistent patterns emerged across cases: (1) labelling and limits to tolerance (where normal dementia behaviors like wandering or calling out were labeled as problematic due to staff's low capacity to manage symptoms), (2) pushing prescription as a solution (where medication, primarily risperidone, was promoted as a first resort rather than last option), (3) coverups and avoiding consent (where medications were administered without family knowledge or consent, including deceptive practices and workarounds like misusing fentanyl patches), and (4) family's fight for liberty (where family members advocated against medication to preserve loved ones' functioning, dignity, and quality of life). The findings indicate that chemical restraint often functions as "behavioural euthanasia" — a term used by families to describe subduing residents for staff convenience rather than therapeutic need — driven by systemic workforce shortco

title: The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention
text: The Right to Choose and Refuse Mental Health Care; A Human Rights Based Approach to Ending Compulsory Psychiatric Intervention Summary This article critically examines how U.S. mental health law systematically denies people with psychosocial disabilities the right to refuse psychiatric treatment while simultaneously restricting access to culturally responsive, community-based care. Drawing on constitutional law, international human rights frameworks, and critical disability theory, Bergan demonstrates that forced psychiatric interventions are justified through ableist and sanist assumptions that question the decision-making capacity of individuals labeled with mental illness. The article contrasts this with the robust legal protections afforded to individuals refusing somatic medical care, even in life-threatening situations, highlighting a discriminatory double standard. Through historical analysis, the author traces psychiatry’s role in pathologizing marginalized groups—including Black, Indigenous, poor, queer, and disabled communities—and argues that compulsory treatment functions as a mechanism of social control rather than therapeutic care. The piece concludes by advocating for a human rights-based approach that upholds bodily autonomy, informed consent, and access to voluntary, culturally responsive services as essential to ending coercive psychiatric practices. title: Utilization of the Fordham Risk Screening Tool for violence risk assessment in an emergency department
text: Utilization of the Fordham Risk Screening Tool for violence risk assessment in an emergency department Summary This study evaluated the Fordham Risk Screening Tool (FRST) for violence risk assessment in adult emergency department patients undergoing psychiatric evaluation. Using a prospective observational design, researchers compared the FRST against the Historical Clinical Risk Management-20, Version 3 (HCR-20 v3) as a criterion standard in 105 patients presenting to Maine Medical Center's emergency department between June and August 2022. The FRST demonstrated strong diagnostic accuracy with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.88, sensitivity of 84%, specificity of 93%, positive predictive value of 87%, and negative predictive value of 91%. Psychometric analysis revealed good internal consistency (KR-20 = 0.79) and unidimensional structure supporting construct validity. However, subgroup analysis indicated significantly reduced performance among involuntarily admitted patients (AUC 0.62 vs. 0.94 in voluntary), suggesting potential limitations in legally coerced populations that warrant cautious interpretation due to small subgroup size. Key Entities Fordham Risk Screening Tool (FRST) (tool): A brief screening tool designed to assess violence risk using readily available information during psychiatric intake, evaluated in this study for emergency department use. Historical Clinical Risk Management-20, Version 3 (HCR-20 V3) (tool): A 20-item

title: Chemical and physical restraint of patients
text: Chemical and physical restraint of patients Summary This study evaluates the level of knowledge among 156 nurses in Portugal regarding physical and chemical restraint of patients using a validated 240-item knowledge questionnaire (KQPPCR). The cross-sectional descriptive study assessed knowledge across four dimensions: patient safety, legal and ethical practice, scientific knowledge, and quality of care. Findings reveal that while 92.3% of nurses view physical restraint as a protective measure for patient safety and 87.2% support its use for fall risk, significant gaps exist in legal and ethical understanding—only 47.4% correctly identified that restraint is not an autonomous nursing act. Nurses demonstrated strongest knowledge in scientific concepts (mean 60/88) but weakest in legal and ethical practice (mean 25/38). Despite 69.2% not viewing restraint as a quality indicator, 76.9% still considered it good practice when necessary, indicating cognitive dissonance. The study concludes that targeted training addressing legal and ethical dimensions, not just technical skills, is needed to improve practice. title: The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care
text: The Use of Coercive Measures in Forensic Psychiatric Care Summary This chapter examines coercive measures across the entire mental health care pathway in England and Wales, from community contact through inpatient care to community discharge. It analyzes both formal coercive mechanisms (detention under the Mental Health Act, physical/mechanical restraint, seclusion, rapid tranquilization) and less tangible forms of coercion such as threats of detention, blanket ward rules, and conditional leave arrangements. The authors present evidence of increasing use of coercive measures over time, including a sixfold rise in Section 136 police detentions and over 50,000 Mental Health Act detentions in 2012/13, while highlighting significant gaps in data collection for certain coercive practices. The chapter emphasizes that informal admission status does not guarantee absence of coercion due to structural and perceptual factors, with studies showing 22-33% of informal patients experiencing coercion equivalent to detained patients. It concludes that coercion exists on a continuum and is influenced by patient factors, service organization, and staff practices, with ongoing tension between legal frameworks like the Mental Capacity Act and Mental Health Act. Key Entities Care Quality Commission (CQC) (organization): Primary regulator publishing annual reports on detention, restraint, and seclusion usage in English mental health services; key data source for trends in coercive measures. title: Physical and Chemical Restraints (an Update)
text: Physical and Chemical Restraints (an Update) Summary This 2020 review article by Guerrero and Mycyk updates clinical guidelines for managing agitation in emergency departments, focusing on the safe and ethical use of physical and chemical restraints. It synthesizes current evidence, regulatory standards from CMS and The Joint Commission, and guidelines from the American College of Emergency Physicians. The authors emphasize de-escalation as the first-line strategy, detail protocols for physical restraint use (including team composition, positioning, and monitoring), and evaluate pharmacological options such as antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, and ketamine for chemical restraint. Special considerations for pediatric and geriatric populations are addressed, highlighting age-related physiological differences and the need for tailored approaches. The paper stresses that restraint should be a last resort, used only when safety is imminently threatened, and must be accompanied by thorough documentation, staff training, and ongoing oversight to prevent harm and ensure accountability. Key Entities Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) (org): Sets federal regulatory standards for restraint use, including training requirements and reporting protocols. The Joint Commission (TJC) (org): Accreditation body that mandates reporting of restraint-related deaths within 24 hours and evaluates compliance during inspections. title: Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 04
text: Coercion and Violence in Mental Health Settings - 04 Summary This chapter examines the perspectives of service users, staff, and informal caregivers regarding coercion and violence in mental health settings. It synthesizes research on how these groups experience and perceive coercive interventions such as involuntary hospitalization, restraint, seclusion, and community treatment orders. The authors highlight a fundamental misalignment in perspectives: service users predominantly view coercion as a violation of autonomy and human rights, triggering emotions like fear, anger, and powerlessness, while staff often regard it as necessary for safety and sometimes therapeutic, albeit with acknowledged harm to therapeutic relationships. Informal caregivers express mixed views, balancing concerns for safety with feelings of guilt, exclusion, and strain on family relationships. The chapter discusses measurement tools like the MacArthur Perceived Coercion Scale (MPCS), Experienced Coercion Scale (ECS), Coercion Experience Scale (CES), and Staff Attitude to Coercion Scale (SACS), noting the lack of equivalent tools for measuring perceptions of violence prevention. It concludes that despite policy advancements like the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UN-CRPD) promoting autonomy and dignity, coercive practices persist due to systemic barriers, insufficient implementation of shared decision-making, and lack of meaningful involvement of service users and informal ca

title: Psychiatric Treatment Tort Liability: Standard of Care in Psychopharmacology, Electroconvulsive Therapy, and Psychotherapy
text: Psychiatric Treatment Tort Liability: Standard of Care in Psychopharmacology, Electroconvulsive Therapy, and Psychotherapy Summary This chapter examines the standard of care in psychiatric practice across three treatment modalities—psychopharmacology, electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), and psychotherapy—as it relates to medical malpractice liability. It emphasizes that liability arises not from unfavorable outcomes but from deviations from what reasonably prudent psychiatrists would do under similar circumstances. The analysis draws on legal precedents, professional guidelines (e.g., from the American Psychiatric Association and Joint Commission), and case law to delineate what constitutes negligent versus acceptable practice. A central thesis is that sound clinical practice often serves as effective risk management, though tensions exist where clinical innovation outpaces legal standards. The chapter underscores informed consent as a recurring, non-negotiable requirement across all modalities, particularly highlighting its critical role in mitigating liability related to tardive dyskinesia, ECT risks, and off-label prescribing. While psychotherapy is noted for its inherent variability and lower likelihood of successful malpractice claims, the text identifies clear boundaries—such as sexual exploitation, breach of confidentiality, and abandonment—that fall outside the standard of care and are actionable in court. Key Entities American Psychiatric Association (APA) (organization

title: Defining chemical restraint A preliminary step towards measurement and quality assessment
text: Defining chemical restraint A preliminary step towards measurement and quality assessment Summary This scoping review addresses the persistent lack of consensus on defining chemical restraint in healthcare and forensic settings. Analyzing 33 sources from 2006–2020, the authors identify significant variability in terminology and conceptualization, with some sources excluding chemical restraint from statistics or using proxies like PRN medication without validation. Drawing on Donabedian’s Structure-Process-Outcome framework, the study proposes an operational definition clarifying that chemical restraint involves medication administered to control behavior, prevent harm, avoid physical restraint/seclusion, or without consent. The definition aims to enable reliable measurement, support quality improvement, and align with human rights goals by distinguishing coercive intent from therapeutic use. title: Legal Issues in Emergency Psychiatry and Involuntary Commitment
text: Legal Issues in Emergency Psychiatry and Involuntary Commitment Summary This chapter examines the legal framework governing psychiatric emergency evaluations and involuntary commitment procedures. It outlines the clinician's dual responsibilities in emergency settings: conducting rapid assessments and determining appropriate dispositions (no treatment, outpatient care, or hospitalization). The text details how duty of care arises through therapeutic relationships or federal statutes like EMTALA, which mandates medical stabilization in emergency departments. Regarding involuntary hospitalization, the chapter traces the historical shift from "need for treatment" standards to danger-based criteria following the 1972 Lessard v. Schmidt decision, establishing that modern commitment laws primarily rely on police powers rather than parens patriae justifications. The analysis covers voluntary admission types (pure and conditional), competence requirements highlighted by Zinermon v. Burch, and procedural safeguards including least restrictive alternative principles. Special attention is given to psychiatric boarding in emergency departments and its legal implications, noting that some courts have ruled prolonged detention unlawful due to inadequate treatment environments. Key Entities Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act (EMTALA) (U.S. federal law) — establishes legal duty for emergency departments to provide urgent care, affecting psychiatric emergency evaluations and risk of ab

title: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights
text: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights Summary This source examines the interpretation of Article 12 of the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), which guarantees the right to legal capacity on an equal basis with others. The author argues that Article 12 must be interpreted through the principle of the indivisibility of human rights, requiring that civil-political rights like legal capacity be understood in conjunction with socio-economic rights and other fundamental values such as dignity and equality. The analysis concludes that while supported decision-making is a primary obligation under Article 12(3), there are 'hard cases'—such as severe cognitive impairment, risk of serious harm, or undue influence—where decision-making by a substitute may be necessary as a last resort to uphold the person's indivisible human rights, particularly dignity and safety. The argument draws on the CRPD's preamble and general comments, disability jurisprudence, philosophical perspectives on personhood (e.g., Nussbaum, Kittay), and illustrative case studies (Alex, Sesha, Jess) to challenge the prevailing view that any substitute decision-making violates Article 12. Instead, it posits that dignity, not autonomy, is the true foundation of personhood for those who cannot exercise autonomous decision-making, even with support, and that denying substitute decision-making in extreme cases may itself violate other rights under the CRPD. title: Ethical challenges in contemporary psychiatry: an overview and an appraisal of possible strategies and research needs
text: Ethical challenges in contemporary psychiatry: an overview and an appraisal of possible strategies and research needs Summary This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of contemporary ethical challenges in psychiatry, identifying key areas where ethical tensions arise despite shared medical ethics foundations. The authors examine how psychiatry's unique power dynamics and societal perceptions create distinct challenges around human rights, coercion, digital technologies, early intervention, end-of-life decisions, conflicts of interest, and lived experience involvement. Drawing on international frameworks including the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), the World Psychiatric Association's Code of Ethics, and empirical evidence, the paper argues that current ethical guidelines are insufficient to address evolving technological and societal changes. The analysis emphasizes that resolving these challenges requires systemic reforms involving policy changes, service redesign, professional training attitudinal shifts, and meaningful collaboration with people with lived experience, rather than relying solely on individual clinician adherence to ethical codes. title: The Role of Psychiatrists in Seclusion and Restraint Episodes: a Curriculum for Psychiatric Trainees
text: The Role of Psychiatrists in Seclusion and Restraint Episodes: a Curriculum for Psychiatric Trainees Summary This study developed and evaluated a 90-minute educational curriculum for psychiatry trainees focusing on their role in seclusion and restraint episodes. The curriculum included a didactic component covering historical context, regulatory requirements, and chemical restraint concepts; an open discussion on ethical considerations and racial disparities; and a case-based discussion. Delivered to psychiatry interns during adolescent psychiatry rotations at an academic hospital, the study used pre- and post-test surveys to assess changes in knowledge and comfort. Results showed significant improvements in both knowledge (from 40% to 87% correct) and self-rated comfort (from 2.7 to 3.9 on a 1–7 scale), with high participant satisfaction and perceived relevance to future practice. Qualitative feedback emphasized the value of safe dialogue and requested more case-based learning. The authors conclude that while the brief intervention shows promise for increasing awareness and readiness, sustained, multi-component training is needed to impact actual clinical practice and address systemic issues like racial disparities in seclusion and restraint use. title: The Conditions for Ethical Chemical Restraints
text: The Conditions for Ethical Chemical Restraints Summary This paper analyzes the ethical permissibility of chemical restraints by distinguishing between interventions that affect consciousness (a basic liberty) versus those that alter only a particular conscious state (not a basic liberty). Drawing on philosophical concepts of liberty and prior work on physical restraint ethics, the authors argue that chemical restraints like propofol—which induce unconsciousness—are morally equivalent to physical restraints and require the same stringent conditions: least liberty-restrictive means, medical appropriateness, and informed consent. In contrast, agents like benzodiazepines or haloperidol that calm without sedating to unconsciousness impose lesser liberty restrictions and thus may be ethically permissible under less stringent conditions, provided they additionally promote the patient’s capacity for rational decision-making once the acute threat subsides. The paper emphasizes that intent matters: using antipsychotics at sedating doses when lower therapeutic doses suffice transforms treatment into restraint, triggering full ethical scrutiny. The framework provides a nuanced approach to evaluating chemical restraints by grading their moral weight based on depth of neurological impact rather than treating all psychotropic interventions as equivalent. Key Entities Joint Commission (organization): Sets standards on restraint and seclusion but does not distinguish between physical and chem

title: Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next? Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation
text: Which Emergent Medication Should I Give Next? Repeated Use of Emergent Medications to Treat Acute Agitation Summary This review addresses the lack of evidence-based guidance for managing persistent acute agitation when an initial pharmacological intervention fails. The authors systematically review existing clinical guidelines, which show inconsistency in first-line recommendations and provide no direction for subsequent steps. They synthesize data from observational studies and randomized controlled trials on medications like droperidol, midazolam, olanzapine, and haloperidol, documenting frequencies of redosing, alternative medication use, and associated adverse events such as extrapyramidal symptoms, respiratory depression, and cardiac events. The paper identifies polypharmacy, QT-prolonging drug combinations, age over 65, alcohol intoxication, and rapid sequential dosing as key risk factors for adverse outcomes. Based on this evidence, the authors propose practical, stepped clinical algorithms for oral/inhalational, intramuscular, and mixed routes of administration, emphasizing calmness over sedation, regular reassessment using standardized agitation scales, avoidance of unnecessary polypharmacy, and consideration of medical contributors to agitation before escalating psychotropics. The proposed algorithms are noted as expert-informed but not empirically validated, highlighting the need for prospective studies specifically evaluating sequential medication strategies. Key

title: Mental health and human rights in the 21st century
text: Mental health and human rights in the 21st century Summary This commentary argues that involuntary psychiatric interventions conflict with international human rights law, particularly the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), which mandates a shift from the medical model to a human rights model of disability. The authors critique the continued use of coercion—such as forced treatment, seclusion, and restraint—despite its lack of efficacy, documented harm, and misallocation of resources toward institutional renovation in wealthy regions like Europe. They reject the idea that capacity assessments can justify coercion as exceptional, arguing such judgments are subjective and risk reinforcing paternalism and discrimination. Instead, they advocate for a fundamental reorientation of psychiatry toward supporting individuals’ will and preferences, with substituted decision-making set aside except in extreme emergencies requiring ongoing dialogue for rights-respecting alternatives. The piece positions the CRPD as a transformative legal instrument that obliges states to end coercive practices and invest in community-based, rights-respecting care, framing this shift as essential for professional legitimacy, social justice, and effective mental health systems. Key Entities Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) (type: framework) — central legal instrument establishing the human rights model of disability and obligating states to end forced tr

title: The WPA Code of Ethics for Psychiatry
text: The WPA Code of Ethics for Psychiatry Summary The World Psychiatric Association (WPA) adopted its first comprehensive Code of Ethics for Psychiatry in October 2020, marking a significant development in global psychiatric ethics after nearly a decade of development. The code was developed by the WPA's Standing Committee on Ethics and Review with input from psychiatric societies worldwide, replacing earlier fragmented ethics declarations such as the Declaration of Madrid (1996) and the Declaration of Hawaii (1977). Structured around five core principles of medical ethics—beneficence, respect for patient autonomy, non-maleficence, improving standards of practice, and applying psychiatric expertise to societal service—the code is organized into four domains: clinical practice, education, research, and public health. It serves multiple functions, including guiding individual psychiatrists, informing public expectations, helping member societies benchmark their ethics, and supporting psychiatrists facing political pressure. The WPA emphasizes that the code is intended to complement, not replace, national ethics codes and is designed as a living document to evolve with advances in psychiatry and societal values. Key Entities World Psychiatric Association (WPA) (type: org) — The international organization that developed and adopted the Code of Ethics for Psychiatry as its official ethical statement. Standing Committee on Ethics and Review (WPA) (type: org) — The WPA committee respons

title: Ethical dilemmas in contemporary psychiatry: Findings from a survey of National Psychiatric Associations in Europe
text: Ethical dilemmas in contemporary psychiatry: Findings from a survey of National Psychiatric Associations in Europe Summary This study surveyed representatives from National Psychiatric Associations (NPAs) across Europe to assess their ethical guidelines, practices, and perspectives on ethical dilemmas in psychiatry. Conducted between 2021 and 2022, the survey achieved a 70.4% response rate, gathering input from 31 NPAs across 30 countries. The research found that in most participating countries, the general mission statement serves as the primary document addressing ethical issues in psychiatry, with only a minority maintaining separate ethical codes. The most frequently addressed ethical topics in internal documents were medical malpractice and discrimination/racism, particularly in professional relationships. While psychiatric expertise is represented in national medical ethics bodies in most countries, only a minority of NPAs have formally adopted international ethical codes such as the Declaration of Helsinki or the WPA Declaration of Madrid. Despite this, a strong majority of respondents endorsed international codes as the primary source of ethical principles and expressed support for sharing national documents to create a European Psychiatric Association (EPA)-supported database of ethical guidelines. title: Comparison of coercive practices in worldwide mental healthcare: overcoming difficulties resulting from variations in monitoring strategies
text: Comparison of coercive practices in worldwide mental healthcare: overcoming difficulties resulting from variations in monitoring strategies Summary This study compared coercive practices across nine high-income countries (Australia, England, Germany, Ireland, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, USA, Wales) using data from WHO, OECD, and national sources. Despite significant variations in how countries define and report restrictive practices—such as seclusion, physical, mechanical, and chemical restraint—the researchers standardized metrics by normalizing for population and using duration data to convert between event counts and time-based measures. The analysis revealed 10- to 100-fold differences in key indicators like mechanical restraints per 100,000 population per year and restrictive practice incidents (RPI) per 1 million population per day, with Japan consistently showing the highest rates across most metrics. Involuntary hospital admission showed less variation (median 41%) and was treated separately due to its distinct ethical and legal character. The findings suggest that cross-national comparison is feasible despite reporting inconsistencies, and that cultural or legal norms—not just clinical need—likely drive national differences in coercion use. Key Entities World Health Organization (WHO) (organization) — Provider of the Mental Health Atlas dataset, which currently only tracks involuntary hospital admissions as a proxy for coercion; the study recommends expanding it

title: Psychiatric Advance Directives Under the Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities: Why Advance Instructions Should Be Able to Override Current Preferences
text: Psychiatric Advance Directives Under the Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities: Why Advance Instructions Should Be Able to Override Current Preferences Summary This article examines how the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) impacts the effectiveness of Psychiatric Advance Directives (PADs). The authors analyze two competing interpretations of the CRPD: a "radical" model endorsed by the Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities that requires absolute respect for current preferences, and a "combined supported decision making model" that integrates supported decision making with competence assessment and substitute decision making. Through clinical vignettes involving schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, they demonstrate that the radical CRPD model renders PADs ineffective precisely when they are most needed—during mental health crises where patients express clear but conflicting treatment preferences. The combined model better supports PAD functionality by allowing advance directives to guide decisions when decision-making capacity is impaired, even if some communication ability remains. The authors conclude that while the radical interpretation undermines PAD utility, a more flexible approach that incorporates capacity assessment can preserve both autonomy and effective crisis intervention. title: The Limitations of Principlism
text: The Limitations of Principlism Summary This commentary critiques the applicability of principlism—a dominant ethical framework in healthcare based on autonomy, beneficence, nonmaleficence, and justice—for guiding decisions about chemical restraints in psychiatric and emergency care. The authors argue that principlism overemphasizes individual autonomy as an isolated, self-sufficient capacity, failing to account for the relational and communal dimensions of ethical decision-making in clinical settings. They contend that principlism lacks a theory of the good to resolve value conflicts, particularly when balancing the agitated patient’s autonomy against the safety and wellbeing of others in the care environment. Drawing on feminist ethics, legal frameworks like the ICCPR, and concepts of relational autonomy and moral community, the authors propose that ethical restraint decisions must consider the impact on the entire milieu of care, including witnesses and staff, not just the patient-clinician dyad. The significance lies in advancing a more socially embedded model of clinical ethics that integrates communal responsibility and relational autonomy to address limitations of individualistic frameworks in real-world, conflict-laden scenarios involving psychotropic medications. Key Entities Emory University School of Medicine (Organization): Affiliation of the authors Jed P. Mangal and Nathan S. Scheiner; central to the source as the institutional base of the commentators. title: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights
text: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights Summary This chapter analyzes the role of dignity in the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), arguing that dignity functions as an inherent legal principle rather than a standalone right. It conducts a textual and comparative analysis of the CRPD alongside foundational human rights instruments like the UDHR, ICCPR, and ICESCR, noting the CRPD’s unusually frequent and central references to dignity in its Preamble, Article 1 (Purpose), and Article 3 (General Principles). The chapter contends that dignity in the CRPD must be understood as a guiding principle for interpreting substantive rights, particularly Article 12 on legal capacity, and proposes an original five-dimensional framework to define its meaning: (1) equal worth or value, (2) recognition of autonomy for those capable of it, (3) recognition of humans as interdependent and social beings, (4) recognition of embodied personhood, and (5) upholding the indivisibility of human rights. This framework aims to counter reductionist views that equate dignity solely with autonomy or rationality, which risk excluding persons with significant cognitive disabilities. The chapter supports its argument with philosophical sources (e.g., Kant on inherent value), legal scholarship (e.g., McCrudden on dignity as a legal principle), and jurisprudence (e.g., Pretty v UK on dignity as the essence of the ECHR), while acknowledging critiques and

title: The Controversy over “Chemical Restraint” in Acute Care Psychiatry
text: The Controversy over “Chemical Restraint” in Acute Care Psychiatry Summary The article examines the clinical, ethical, and regulatory controversy surrounding the use of medication to control behavior in psychiatric emergencies, termed "chemical restraint." It highlights the lack of consensus on whether such interventions constitute coercion or necessary treatment, noting that regulatory definitions from CMS and JCAHO are imprecise and hinge on the subjective determination of therapeutic intent versus behavioral control. Drawing on expert consensus guidelines, clinical data, and utilization estimates, the author finds that approximately 16% of psychiatric emergency patients receive involuntary medication, projecting over 135,000 annual cases in the U.S., and notes a clinical shift from typical to atypical antipsychotics (e.g., ziprasidone, olanzapine) due to improved side-effect profiles. The article emphasizes that decisions to use medication are influenced by patient factors (e.g., cognitive impairment), institutional culture, staff attitudes, and systemic pressures like overcrowding, while advocating for de-escalation and patient preference as preferred first steps. It concludes that oversight remains inadequate, particularly in non-healthcare settings such as jails and shelters, where coercive practices may serve punitive rather than therapeutic purposes. Key Entities Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) (type: org) — defines chemical restraint as any drug used

title: An introduction to clinical ethics in psychiatry
text: An introduction to clinical ethics in psychiatry Summary This article examines ethical challenges in psychiatric practice through analysis of key legal cases (Re C, RM v St Andrew’s Healthcare, Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board) and builds upon the Royal College of Psychiatrists’ CPD module on clinical ethics. It critiques the limitations of the four principles approach (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) in psychiatry due to ambiguous interpretation, conflicting priorities, and difficulties in factual assessment—particularly regarding future risk predictions and personal identity. The authors argue that ethical dilemmas are exacerbated by questions of personal identity in chronic mental illness and the difficulty of distinguishing illness-expressed values from the patient’s authentic self. When clinical ethics committees are unavailable (noting only three exist in UK NHS Mental Health Trusts), psychiatrists must engage in reflective practice, recognize personal and institutional biases, prioritize dialogue, and use Socratic questioning to navigate value conflicts. The article emphasizes that informed consent requires not just risk disclosure but a genuine exploration of what matters to the patient, and that deception (e.g., covert medication) undermines trust and justice regardless of intent or outcome. title: A Human Rights-Based Approach to Psychiatry; Is It Possible?
text: A Human Rights-Based Approach to Psychiatry; Is It Possible? Summary This paper evaluates a pilot project (2012–2015) that applied a human rights-based approach (HRBA) to psychiatric care in Region Västra Götaland, Sweden. The initiative involved three sites—psychiatric services at Sahlgrenska University Hospital, Angered Hospital, and Bohusläns Museum—and aimed to integrate HRBA principles (dignity and empowerment, equality and non-discrimination, participation and inclusion, accountability and transparency) into organizational practice. The psychiatric component focused on reducing coercive measures, particularly belt restraints, through staff training, patient involvement, and organizational changes such as establishing a user council and revising non-therapeutic ward rules. Outcomes included a reduction in belt use from approximately four times per month to four times per year on Ward 242, increased staff capacity to question coercion, improved well-being for patients and staff, and the adoption of a regional "zero vision" for coercive measures in the 2017–2020 human rights action plan. The authors emphasize that success depended on early involvement of rights holders, dedicated time for reflection, leadership commitment, and linking HRBA to existing quality improvement efforts. title: Definition and Measurement of Physical and Chemical Restraint in Long-Term Care; A Systematic Review
text: Definition and Measurement of Physical and Chemical Restraint in Long-Term Care; A Systematic Review Summary This systematic review analyzed 86 studies on physical restraint (20 of which also addressed chemical restraint) in long-term care settings to examine how restraint is defined and measured. The researchers extracted both explicit definitions (verbatim statements) and implicit definitions (derived from measurement approaches or result reporting) and conducted a thematic content analysis. Seven core themes emerged across definitions: restraint method, setting where restraint occurs, stated intent, resident capacity to remove or control the restraint, caveats and exclusions, duration/frequency/number, and consent and resistance. The review found substantial variability in how these themes were incorporated into explicit definitions, and even greater inconsistency between explicit definitions and how restraint was actually measured or reported. Notably, while many explicit definitions included resident capacity to remove/control and stated intent, measurement tools like the RAI-MDS frequently omitted these elements. Conversely, implicit definitions from data collection often captured duration and setting but missed intent and consent. No studies reported validity of their measurement approaches, and only a minority reported inter-rater reliability. The review concludes that the lack of alignment between definition, measurement, and reporting undermines the validity of rest

title: Reducing Involuntary Admissions
text: Reducing Involuntary Admissions Summary This chapter examines involuntary admission in mental health settings through legal, ethical, clinical, and experiential lenses. It analyzes how the practice persists despite widespread ratification of the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), which many interpret as prohibiting deprivation of liberty based on disability. The authors review global variation in admission rates, pathways to detention, and subjective experiences of patients, staff, and caregivers, highlighting tensions between public safety, clinical judgment, and human rights. The chapter evaluates reform efforts, particularly psychiatric advance directives (PADs), joint crisis plans (JCPs), and supported decision-making, presenting empirical evidence that these approaches can reduce reliance on coercion while maintaining safety and treatment engagement. title: Mental Health as a Basic Human Right and the Interference of Commercialized Science
text: Mental Health as a Basic Human Right and the Interference of Commercialized Science Summary This paper argues that a robust rights-based approach to mental health is essential to counter the detrimental effects of commercialized science in psychiatry, where industry influence distorts research, clinical guidelines, and medical education to prioritize profit over public health. The authors critique the dominance of the biomedical model—particularly as operationalized in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM)—for reducing mental distress to individual biological dysfunction while ignoring social, economic, and political determinants. They demonstrate how pharmaceutical industry funding biases psychiatric research, with industry-sponsored trials more likely to report favorable outcomes, and how financial conflicts of interest are pervasive among DSM panel members and clinical guideline authors. Furthermore, industry influence on continuing medical education promotes prescribing practices that favor expensive, patented medications over cheaper, equally effective alternatives. The paper contends that this epistemic narrowing undermines core human rights principles such as participation, autonomy, and equitable access to care, and advocates for structural interventions—like housing, anti-discrimination policies, and lived-experience leadership—as necessary components of a truly rights-respecting mental health system. title: Violence Risk Assessment
text: Violence Risk Assessment Summary This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of violence risk assessment (VRA) in mental health settings, tracing its evolution from unstructured professional judgment to structured approaches that emphasize prevention over prediction. It examines three primary methodologies: unstructured professional judgment (UPJ), actuarial decision-making, and structured professional judgment (SPJ), comparing their strengths and limitations. The chapter introduces the Early Recognition Method (ERM) as a proactive, client-centered strategy that identifies and manages early warning signs of aggression through four phases, supported by tools like the Forensic Early Signs of Aggression Inventory (FESAI). Special attention is given to the distinction between static and dynamic risk factors, the importance of protective factors, and the significant contributions of staff, interpersonal interactions, and environmental factors to inpatient violence—particularly noting that 40% of incidents involve staff-patient interactions. The chapter also addresses the challenges of applying standard risk assessment tools to special populations such as individuals with intellectual disability and autism spectrum disorder, advocating for tailored approaches. Ultimately, it argues for a paradigm shift toward organizational innovations like the High Intensive Care model, which prioritizes autonomy, low coercion, and therapeutic environments to reduce aggression and the need for

title: Chapter 6: Seclusion and Restraint
text: Chapter 6: Seclusion and Restraint Summary This chapter examines the legal and clinical framework governing the use of seclusion and restraint in psychiatric settings. It defines seclusion as involuntary confinement alone in a room and restraint as the application of physical force—including drugs—to restrict movement. The chapter emphasizes that these interventions must be used only as a last resort when less restrictive measures fail and to prevent imminent harm to self or others, not for staff convenience or punishment. It details the overlapping regulatory landscape involving CMS, JCAHO, state laws, and professional guidelines, highlighting requirements such as physician evaluation within one hour (per CMS), documentation, continuous monitoring, and due-process procedures including notice and impartial review. Landmark cases like Youngberg v. Romeo are discussed, acknowledging that restraint may rarely be justified for training but emphasizing the primacy of safety. The chapter also outlines clinical indications, contraindications (e.g., in delirium or dementia), and risks of misuse, supported by malpractice cases such as Fleming and Pisel, which illustrate liability for improper use. Key Entities Center for Medicare Services (CMS): organization — sets federal conditions of participation for Medicare/Medicaid hospitals, including 1-hour physician review requirement for seclusion/restraint. Joint Commission on Accreditation of Healthcare Organizations (JCAHO): organization

title: Seventy Years of Coercion in the German Psychiatric System, Experienced and Witnessed
text: Seventy Years of Coercion in the German Psychiatric System, Experienced and Witnessed Summary Dorothea Buck-Zerchin's testimony documents her lived experience of coercive psychiatric treatment in Germany spanning seven decades, from forced sterilization at age 19 in 1936 to ongoing pressures into the 1970s. She describes inhumane practices at institutions like the von Bodelschwinghsche Asylum Bethel, including prolonged restraint, wet packs, and forced medication, justified by a biological reductionist framework rooted in Emil Kraepelin's legacy. Her account connects psychiatric coercion to eugenic ideologies, noting Protestant advocacy for sterilization predating Nazism and postwar resistance to compensation. Buck-Zerchin contrasts these practices with humane alternatives such as Soteria and Finland's need-adapted treatment, which prioritize dialogue and psychosocial support. Her recovery after five psychotic episodes—achieved by understanding psychosis as meaningful response to life crises—challenges the chronicity model of schizophrenia. She further details the development of peer-led initiatives like Psychosis Seminars and Experienced Involvement (EX-IN), which embody trialogue principles and affirm survivor expertise. The testimony argues that coercive psychiatry persists when patient voice is excluded, advocating for an empirical science grounded in lived experience. Key Entities German Federal Association of (ex-)Users and Survivors of Psychiatry (type: org) — national

title: Ethical and Legal Concerns Associated With Withdrawing Mechanical Circulatory Support; A U.S. Perspective
text: Ethical and Legal Concerns Associated With Withdrawing Mechanical Circulatory Support; A U.S. Perspective Summary This article examines the ethical and legal permissibility of withdrawing mechanical circulatory support (MCS) devices—such as ventricular assist devices (VADs), total artificial hearts (TAHs), and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO)—in the United States. Drawing on U.S. legal precedents, clinical case series, and bioethical principles, the author argues that withdrawing MCS at the request of an informed patient or surrogate is permissible when the intent is to remove a burdensome life-sustaining treatment, not to cause death. The analysis emphasizes that death following MCS withdrawal results from the underlying cardiovascular disease, not the withdrawal itself, thereby distinguishing it from physician-assisted suicide or euthanasia. Clinical evidence from Mayo Clinic case series indicates that most MCS withdrawals occur after multidisciplinary care conferences, involve surrogate decision-makers (often due to lack of patient decisional capacity), and result in death within a day, consistent with natural disease progression. The article underscores the importance of advance care planning to address MCS-specific wishes, as few current advance directives mention these technologies.
