title: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights
text: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights Summary This chapter examines the evolution of disability models and their implications for interpreting Article 12 of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), which concerns equal recognition before the law and legal capacity. It traces the historical development from charity and medical models to the social model, noting how the CRPD was initially hailed as embodying social model principles. However, the author critiques strict interpretations of the social model for failing to account for the lived experience of impairment, especially in cognitive disability where intrinsic limitations interact with social barriers. The chapter further analyzes the human rights model of disability as promoted in UN General Comments, arguing that while it attempts to address shortcomings of the social model by affirming impairment as part of identity, it contains an internal contradiction by asserting universal legal capacity without adequately addressing how cognitive impairment affects decision-making capacity. Supported decision-making is presented as an evolving alternative to substituted decision-making under CRPD Article 12, aiming to preserve autonomy while providing necessary support. Key Entities Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) (org): The central international treaty governing disability rights, particularly Article 12 on legal capacity and supported decision-m

title: Human Rights Violations and Mental Illness
text: Human Rights Violations and Mental Illness Summary This source reviews global literature on human rights violations experienced by individuals with mental illness both within psychiatric institutions and in community settings. The authors conducted an extensive analysis of peer-reviewed articles, policy papers, and government publications from 2000 onward, identifying violations such as denial of employment, marriage, procreation, education, malnutrition, physical abuse, and neglect. The review links these violations to systemic drivers including stigmatization, inadequate mental health legislation, severe resource shortages (e.g., Ethiopia's 18 psychiatrists for 77 million people), and cultural factors that influence help-seeking behaviors. The paper argues that such violations create barriers to treatment adherence, as individuals may avoid care due to fear of abuse or marginalization, thereby exacerbating mental health conditions and perpetuating cycles of disadvantage. The authors emphasize the need for legislative reform, increased investment in mental health workforce, culturally responsive approaches, and meaningful engagement with affected communities to uphold rights and improve outcomes. Key Entities World Health Organization (WHO) (org: org) — cited for global mental health burden estimates, human rights project, and policy frameworks United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD) (org/tool: org) — discussed as key legislative framewo

title: Use of a Novel Standardized Administration Protocol Reduces Agitation Pro Re Nata (PRN) Medication Requirements: The Birmingham Agitation Management (BAM)
text: Use of a Novel Standardized Administration Protocol Reduces Agitation Pro Re Nata (PRN) Medication Requirements: The Birmingham Agitation Management (BAM) Summary This study evaluated the Birmingham Agitation Management (BAM) protocol, which links the Brøset Violence Checklist (BVC) to a standardized PRN medication order set in an inpatient psychiatric unit. Researchers compared PRN administration rates before and after implementing BAM across 377 patients (184 pre-BAM, 193 BAM) at a U.S. medical center. The intervention standardized care by linking BVC scores (0–6) to specific medication actions: no medication for score 0, low-dose benzodiazepine for 1, high-dose benzodiazepine for 2, and high-dose benzodiazepine plus antipsychotic for scores ≥3. Results showed a 42.5% reduction in total PRN administrations, with mean doses per patient dropping from 8.21 to 4.39 (p<0.001) and median doses also significantly reduced. Notably, 40% of BAM patients received zero PRNs versus 22.8% pre-BAM. Crucially, there were no significant increases in seclusion (p=0.124) or restraint (p=0.994) duration, indicating reduced medication use did not increase reliance on coercive measures. The study suggests that standardizing the process of PRN administration—through objective scoring and clear action links—may be more impactful than medication choice alone for reducing inappropriate use. Key Entities Birmingham Agitation Management (BAM) (tool/protocol) — The intervention studied; a standardized

title: Protocol for the management of psychiatric patients with psychomotor agitation
text: Protocol for the management of psychiatric patients with psychomotor agitation Summary This source describes a clinical protocol developed by a multidisciplinary expert panel in Spain for the assessment and management of psychiatric patients experiencing psychomotor agitation (PMA). The protocol integrates international consensus guidelines with local Spanish healthcare standards and hospital-specific Standard Operating Procedures through a formal consensus process using the Delphi method. It provides three clinical algorithms for initial assessment, pharmacological intervention selection, and physical restraint standards, advocating a stepwise approach that prioritizes safety, environmental modifications, verbal de-escalation, and pharmacological treatment before considering seclusion or restraint as last resorts. The protocol aims to standardize care, promote early intervention, reduce reliance on coercive measures, and ensure patient and staff safety through structured, evidence-informed guidance. Key Entities Hospital Clínic de Barcelona (type: org) — Hosted the expert panel and provided institutional support for protocol development Center of Biomedical Network Research on Mental Health (CIBERSAM) (type: org) — Provided input and endorsement on the protocol as a national mental health research network Catalan Society of Psychiatry and Mental Health (type: org) — Reviewed and provided feedback on the protocol during development Spanish Society of Biological Psychiatry (SE

title: Elimination of restrictive practices from acute adult mental health care services; A qualitative evidence synthesis of the lived experience literature
text: Elimination of restrictive practices from acute adult mental health care services; A qualitative evidence synthesis of the lived experience literature Summary This study conducted a qualitative evidence synthesis (QES) of 44 qualitative studies published between 1995 and 2023 to synthesize the lived experiences of mental health service users and practitioners regarding restrictive practices in adult acute mental health care. Using PRISMA 2020 guidelines and the Covidence platform for study selection, the research applied inductive thematic synthesis via NVivo to analyze first-order participant quotes and second-order themes from the included studies. Study quality was assessed using the CASP Qualitative Checklist, with most articles rated as high or moderate quality. The synthesis generated five overarching themes: (1) Meaning and emotional experience, (2) Re-traumatisation and dehumanisation, (3) Professional competencies and varying experiences, (4) Balancing safety versus care, and (5) Alternatives to restrictive practices. The findings highlight that restrictive practices are frequently experienced as traumatic, dehumanizing, and emotionally distressing, often triggering past trauma and evoking fear, shame, and powerlessness. Despite this, some staff perceive them as necessary for safety, reflecting a tension between custodial control and therapeutic care. The study concludes that elimination requires addressing staff attitudes, improving communication and de-escalation s

title: Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings
text: Prevalence and variability of restrictive care practice use (physical restraint, seclusion and chemical restraint) in adult mental health inpatient settings Summary This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesized evidence from 77 studies published between January 2010 and August 2022 to estimate the pooled prevalence of restrictive care practices (RCPs) in adult mental health inpatient settings and identify sources of variability in reported estimates. Using PRISMA 2020 guidelines, the study analyzed data from Scopus, MEDLINE, PsycINFO, Web of Science, Embase, and CINAHL databases. The pooled prevalence estimates were 14.4% for physical restraint, 15.8% for seclusion, and 25.7% for chemical restraint. However, the data exhibited extreme heterogeneity, with I² statistics exceeding 99% for all outcomes, indicating substantial inconsistency across studies. Meta-regression revealed that geographical location and data collection methods significantly explained variability in prevalence estimates, with Asian studies reporting higher rates and direct observation yielding higher estimates than medical record review. Study design, definition specificity, and restraint type did not significantly account for variability. The findings underscore the need for standardized definitions and measurement protocols to enable valid cross-regional comparisons and evaluation of reduction initiatives. Key Entities Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) (org) — Used for risk of bias assessment via JBI C

title: Eastern and Western Approaches to Coercion in Mental Health
text: Eastern and Western Approaches to Coercion in Mental Health Summary This chapter examines coercion in mental health care through a cross-cultural lens, comparing Western and Eastern perspectives. It defines coercion as the exercise of power to influence behavior, potentially through force or coercion when persuasion fails, and situates it within global human rights frameworks like the UNCRPD. The authors present epidemiological data showing high rates of coercive practices internationally—ranging from 3% in Portugal to 60% in England for involuntary admission—and highlight that coercion remains prevalent despite ethical concerns. In India, 63% of psychiatric patients experienced some form of restraint, with 27% receiving intravenous injections when staff were the target of aggression. The chapter emphasizes that coercion damages therapeutic relationships and patient dignity, particularly through perceived coercion, which predicts poorer patient–therapist relationships and is influenced by factors such as gender, religion, income, and illness insight. It argues that reducing coercion requires sustained, multi-faceted efforts including organizational changes, staff training, risk assessment, environmental modifications, psychotherapy, debriefings, and advance directives. Notably, patients who regain insight often retrospectively justify involuntary treatment, suggesting that effective care can mitigate coercive experiences. The chapter concludes by discussing the Mysore Declara

title: Patients' experiences with coercive mental health treatment in Flexible Assertive Community Treatment a qualitative study
text: Patients' experiences with coercive mental health treatment in Flexible Assertive Community Treatment a qualitative study Summary This qualitative study explored how service users experience coercion within Flexible Assertive Community Treatment (FACT) teams in Norway compared to prior treatment contexts. Using a participatory approach, researchers conducted 24 semi-structured interviews with individuals who had experienced coercion and were engaged with five different FACT teams. Thematic analysis revealed three distinct patterns of experience: some participants viewed FACT as a clear improvement offering greater autonomy and less top-down control; others described "making the best of FACT" by complying to avoid worse outcomes like forced medication; and a third group found coercion equally distressing in FACT as before, particularly due to forced medication and systemic distrust. Across narratives, participants identified feeling safe (through accessible crisis support), improved treatment quality (via team collaboration and tailored medication), and increased participation in care planning as key factors that reduced coercive experiences. Key Entities Flexible Assertive Community Treatment (FACT) (intervention/model): Community-based mental health service model providing flexible, intensive support to individuals with severe mental illness, often including substance use comorbidities, with goals of reducing coercion through recovery-oriented, person-centred care. title: Psychiatric Coercion: Ethical Conditions and Empirical Data
text: Psychiatric Coercion: Ethical Conditions and Empirical Data Summary This chapter examines the ethical justification for psychiatric coercion through four core conditions: benefit to the person, least restrictive means as a last resort, therapeutic effectiveness of treatment, and restoration of autonomy. Drawing on systematic reviews, meta-analyses, emulation studies, and qualitative syntheses, it evaluates these conditions against empirical evidence. The author finds that coercion fails to benefit the majority of those subjected to it, with clinical outcomes showing deterioration on the Health of the Nation Outcome Scale (HoNOS) and patient reports indicating trauma, distress, and eroded trust. Alternatives such as de-escalation training, open wards, home treatment (e.g., Crisis Resolution Teams), peer respites, and crisis cafés exist but are underutilized. Psychiatric interventions show only small effect sizes (Cohen’s d ~0.34–0.36), with antidepressant benefits limited to ~15% of users, falling short of moderate effectiveness. There is no reliable evidence that coercion restores autonomy, with philosophical and neuroscientific perspectives questioning whether meaningful autonomy exists to be restored. The evidence consistently challenges the ethical foundations of psychiatric coercion across multiple methodologies. Key Entities German Ethics Council (org): establishes that benefit to the person concerned is the primary ethical justification for psychiatric coercion, requiri

title: Human Rights and Adherence to Treatment in Patients with Severe Mental Illness: Mental Healthcare Act 2017 Perspective
text: Human Rights and Adherence to Treatment in Patients with Severe Mental Illness: Mental Healthcare Act 2017 Perspective Summary This prospective, comparative study examined the impact of India's Mental Healthcare Act (MHCA) 2017 on medication adherence and human rights awareness among patients with severe mental illness (SMI). Researchers assessed 65 hospitalized patients diagnosed with SMI, dividing them into independent admission groups (IAG) based on decision-making capacity and supported admission groups (SAG) for those lacking capacity or requiring high support. Baseline assessments included sociodemographic data, psychopathology (BPRS), functioning (GAF), insight (SAI-E), adherence (MARS and CRS), and human rights awareness via a Needs Assessment Questionnaire covering physical, emotional, social, religious, and ethical domains. Follow-up assessments occurred at discharge and weeks 1 and 6 post-discharge. The study found no significant differences in psychopathology or functioning between groups over time, but IAG demonstrated significantly higher insight and adherence at baseline. Crucially, the emotional needs domain of human rights awareness was significantly lower in SAG compared to IAG at both baseline and week 6 follow-up. A positive correlation emerged between medication adherence (MARS) and human rights awareness in IAG at baseline (r = 0.4, p < 0.05), while SAG showed a higher correlation coefficient (r = 0.7) that lacked statistical significance in reporting. title: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights - Chapter 6: Article 12 and Equality
text: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights - Chapter 6: Article 12 and Equality Summary This chapter examines how Article 12 of the CRPD, which guarantees legal capacity "on an equal basis with others," should be interpreted in light of equality principles. It argues against formal equality models that demand identical treatment, showing how such approaches fail persons with cognitive disabilities like Jess, whose autonomous choice to remain in unsafe conditions leads to neglect. Drawing on the UN Disability Committee's General Comment No 6 (2018), the chapter proposes that equality under Article 12 requires substantive equality through the four-dimensional framework of inclusive equality: redistributive, recognition, participative, and accommodating dimensions. This framework justifies supported decision-making as the preferred approach and allows substitute decision-making only as a last resort when necessary to prevent grave harm, thereby balancing autonomy with dignity and safety. Key Entities UN Disability Committee (type: org) — Author of General Comment No 6, providing authoritative interpretation of CRPD equality provisions United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) (type: org/framework) — The international human rights treaty establishing rights for persons with disabilities, including Article 12 on legal capacity General Comment No 6 (2018) on equality and non-discrimination (type: org/document) — Authoritat

title: Voluntary and Involuntary Psychiatric Hospitalization
text: Voluntary and Involuntary Psychiatric Hospitalization Summary This chapter examines the legal frameworks governing voluntary and involuntary psychiatric hospitalization in the United States, tracing the evolution of civil commitment standards from historical practices to contemporary applications. It analyzes key Supreme Court cases including Addington v. Texas (which established the "clear and convincing" standard of proof), Zinermon v. Burch (addressing competency for voluntary admission), and O'Connor v. Donaldson (limiting confinement of non-dangerous individuals). The text details procedural and substantive due process requirements, distinguishing between emergency commitment (short-term crisis holds), civil commitment (court-ordered inpatient treatment), and involuntary outpatient commitment (court-ordered community treatment). It emphasizes that civil commitment requires proof of mental illness, dangerousness or grave disability, and absence of less restrictive alternatives, while noting ongoing tensions between state parens patriae and police powers, variability in state laws, and challenges in implementing outpatient programs due to resource constraints. title: Ethical dilemmas in psychiatry; When teams disagree
text: Ethical dilemmas in psychiatry; When teams disagree Summary This paper examines a case involving a patient with treatment-resistant schizophrenia who refused oral medication, leading to a proposal for nasogastric clozapine administration under physical restraint. Despite legal authorization under the Mental Health Act 1983 and ethical justification via weak paternalism and the doctrine of double effect, the clinical team experienced significant internal disagreement rooted in emotional responses such as fear, disgust, and moral conviction. The authors argue that ethical decision-making in such contexts is not primarily driven by rational deliberation but by intuitive, emotion-laden judgments, as described by Haidt’s social intuitionist model. Resolution emerged not through new evidence but through reflective practice, shared deliberation, and social persuasion—particularly when senior figures reframed inaction as harmful. Over time, repeated exposure and team reflection reduced initial resistance, making similar interventions less contentious. The case underscores that ethical legitimacy does not eliminate moral distress and that effective ethical reasoning in teams requires attending to group dynamics, emotional awareness, and value pluralism. title: Coercion and trust in psychiatry: the ultimate contradiction
text: Coercion and trust in psychiatry: the ultimate contradiction Summary This paper presents a survivor-centered critique of coercion in psychiatry, arguing that forced treatment is fundamentally incompatible with trust, genuine care, and human rights. Drawing on philosophical, legal, and empirical evidence, the author contends that coercion violates autonomy and dignity, undermines the therapeutic alliance, and contradicts international human rights standards such as the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD). The analysis critiques paternalism as an ethically flawed justification for overriding patient consent and highlights systemic abuses documented in psychiatric settings worldwide. The paper emphasizes survivor knowledge as a valid epistemology and calls for a paradigm shift toward supported decision-making and non-coercive crisis alternatives. Key Entities International Journal of Human Rights in Healthcare (type: org) — The peer-reviewed journal that published this viewpoint article, providing the academic venue for the author’s critique. UN CRPD (type: org) — The United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, cited as the international human rights framework that prohibits deprivation of legal capacity and underpins the argument against forced psychiatric treatment. MindFreedom International (type: org) — An advocacy organization referenced for its critique of coercive psychiatry, particularly through David Oaks’ characteri

title: Mental health professionals’ feelings and attitudes towards coercion
text: Mental health professionals’ feelings and attitudes towards coercion Summary This study investigated the feelings and attitudes of mental health professionals toward coercion in psychiatric care through a survey of 130 staff at Lausanne University Hospital. Using exploratory structural equation modeling (ESEM), the researchers identified four underlying dimensions shaping professionals’ experiences: internal pressure (ethical conflict and doubt about usefulness), emotional impact (stress, lasting memories, loneliness), external pressure (organizational and situational drivers like lack of time or legal fears), and relational involvement (empathy, debriefing, considering patient preferences). While 34.6% agreed coercion violates fundamental rights, a substantial minority also endorsed its necessity and benefit—42.3% found it indispensable, 35.4% beneficial, and 59.2% believed it does not hinder recovery. Key associations showed that recent exposure to coercion reduced internal pressure (suggesting habituation), psychiatrists reported higher emotional impact, and social workers experienced less external pressure. Having a relative with mental illness intensified emotional responses. The findings highlight a persistent gap between ethical concerns and continued reliance on coercion, even without strong evidence for its efficacy. title: The Lived Experience of Coercion
text: The Lived Experience of Coercion Summary This chapter presents a collection of firsthand testimonies from 21 service users and 2 carers who experienced violence and coercion in UK mental health services. The research involved trust-building interviews designed to minimize re-traumatization while gathering detailed accounts of institutional violence. The authors documented 754 individual incidents across categories including physical restraint, forced medication, forced movement, verbal abuse, implied threats, deliberate neglect, and threats to falsify records. The work challenges the prevailing focus on patient-to-staff violence by highlighting the underacknowledged problem of staff-perpetrated violence, arguing that systemic factors like power differentials, diagnostic overshadowing, and institutional betrayal enable and perpetuate such abuse despite most staff being non-abusive. Key Entities National Health Service (NHS) (type: org) — The healthcare system where the documented incidents occurred, providing the institutional context for mental health service delivery in the UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) (type: org) — Referenced for its guidelines on managing violence and therapeutic approaches, which the authors note include relevant principles for preventing patient harm Health and Safety Executive (type: org) — Cited for prosecuting an NHS Trust over patient deaths related to abuse, illustrating regulatory oversight mechanisms Winterbourne Vie

title: Experiences with legally mandated treatment in patients with schizophrenia: A systematic review of qualitative studies
text: Experiences with legally mandated treatment in patients with schizophrenia: A systematic review of qualitative studies Summary This systematic review synthesized qualitative data from 18 studies involving 401 patients with schizophrenia or related disorders across 10 countries to explore experiences with legally mandated treatment. The review found that patient satisfaction was closely tied to perceived respect for autonomy, with positive experiences including regained independence and recognition of treatment benefits such as symptom control and safety. Negative experiences centered on restrictions to freedom, privacy, and lifestyle, often likened to being jailed, and were exacerbated by poor communication, lack of information, and feeling excluded from decision-making. While many patients retrospectively acknowledged treatment as beneficial—particularly after stabilization—this did not negate contemporaneous feelings of coercion or distress. The findings highlight that ethical legitimacy of legally mandated treatment depends not just on clinical outcomes but on how it is implemented, especially regarding communication, autonomy support, and therapeutic relationships. Key Entities Community Treatment Orders (CTO) (tool/intervention) — A form of legally mandated treatment studied in multiple included papers (e.g., Gibbs et al., Stroud et al.), allowing patients to live in the community under conditions; associated with patient reports of gradual independence and responsibilit

title: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights
text: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights Summary This chapter concludes the book by synthesizing its central argument about interpreting Article 12 of the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD). The author argues that legal capacity under Article 12 must be understood through the principle of indivisibility of human rights, which holds that civil-political and socio-economic rights are interdependent and equally important. Drawing on disability theory, jurisprudence (such as Grootboom and D v UK), and real-world cases like the NDIS implementation in Australia, the chapter contends that a strict focus on autonomy and supported decision-making fails in "hard cases" involving severe or profound cognitive disability. Instead, the author advocates for a balanced approach that permits substitute decision-making as a last resort when supported decision-making cannot ensure safety, dignity, or rights enjoyment, grounding this in a five-dimensional model of dignity and substantive equality. Key Entities United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) (type: treaty) — the central international human rights treaty governing disability rights, especially Article 12 on legal capacity UN Disability Committee (Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities) (type: organization) — the UN body that provides authoritative interpretations of the CRPD, notably advocating for abolition of substitute decision-ma

title: Involuntary Medications
text: Involuntary Medications Summary This source examines the legal and ethical framework governing involuntary medication of individuals with mental illness in correctional and civil commitment settings. It traces the historical shift from beneficence-driven to autonomy-driven models of care, highlighting how patient rights to refuse treatment have evolved through key court cases. The analysis focuses on constitutional protections involving due process, liberty interests, and the balance between individual autonomy and state interests such as parens patriae and police power. Landmark cases including Washington v. Harper, Rennie v. Klein, and Rogers v. Okin are analyzed to illustrate divergent standards for overriding treatment refusal, particularly regarding the sufficiency of administrative versus judicial review processes. The source emphasizes that while involuntary medication may be legally permissible under certain conditions, improper administration carries significant risks including malpractice suits, civil rights violations, and professional sanctions. Harper case) Special Offender Center (SOC) (organization: unit within Washington State Department of Corrections

title: Chemical restraint; A qualitative synthesis review of adult service user and staff experiences in mental health settings
text: Chemical restraint; A qualitative synthesis review of adult service user and staff experiences in mental health settings Summary This qualitative synthesis review analyzed 17 studies from 1996 to 2020 to explore mental health service user and staff experiences of chemical restraint. Using databases including PsycINFO, CINAHL, MEDLINE, Embase, Emcare, Web of Science, and Scopus, the authors applied CASP for critical appraisal and Thomas and Harden's (2008) Thematic Synthesis for data analysis. Three core themes emerged: "Unjustified versus justified," "Violence versus necessity," and "Reflecting back: Positives and negatives." Service users predominantly described chemical restraint as an unjustified, violent act akin to punishment or assault, often reporting fear, humiliation, and powerlessness. In contrast, staff generally viewed it as a justified, necessary response to ensure safety, citing systemic pressures such as understaffing and lack of alternatives. Some service users retrospectively acknowledged chemical restraint as less distressing than other coercive forms, particularly in emergency department contexts, highlighting experiential nuances. Key Entities PsycINFO (tool): Database used for literature search to identify qualitative studies on chemical restraint experiences. CINAHL (tool): Database searched to capture nursing and allied health literature relevant to mental health restraint practices. MEDLINE (tool): Biomedical database queried to include clinical and ps

title: The Utilization of a Rapid Agitation Scale and Treatment Protocol for Patient and Staff Safety in an Inpatient Psychiatric Setting
text: The Utilization of a Rapid Agitation Scale and Treatment Protocol for Patient and Staff Safety in an Inpatient Psychiatric Setting Summary This study evaluated the implementation of the Modified Agitation Severity Scale (MASS) and its associated treatment protocol in an adult inpatient psychiatric setting to assess impact on patient and staff safety. The MASS was developed by modifying an existing agitation severity scale through item reorganization, binary scoring, and clinical weighting to enable rapid (<1 minute) assessment, then integrated into the electronic medical record (EMR) as a physician-ordered protocol guiding behavioral and pharmacological interventions based on score ranges. Over a three-month intervention phase compared to baseline, researchers analyzed EMR data from 742 patients along with staff and patient surveys. The MASS demonstrated strong convergent validity with the Clinical Global Impressions-Aggression Scale (CGI-A, r=0.75), showed no significant increase in nurse burnout despite added twice-daily scoring, and revealed a 44% reduction in average restraint duration per incident (from 18 to 10 minutes) over 18 months post-implementation, though seclusion events did not significantly change. Notably, physicians continued to order the MASS protocol for 55% of patients after the study ended, indicating sustained clinical utility. Key Entities University of Oklahoma (org): Affiliated institution where researchers from the School of Community Medicine and H

title: Safety and Security: A Delicate Balance
text: Safety and Security: A Delicate Balance Summary This chapter examines safety and security in mental health settings through a multidimensional framework that distinguishes between safety (absence of harm) and security (protection against deliberate threats). It argues that traditional approaches focusing narrowly on procedural and environmental controls—such as locked doors, CCTV, and zero-tolerance policies—are insufficient and can undermine therapeutic relationships. Drawing on safety science concepts like Safety-I (reactive, incident reduction) and Safety-II (proactive, learning from success), the authors advocate for a shift toward relational safety and security, emphasizing trust, communication, and mutual respect between service users and staff. The chapter evaluates organizational models—High Intensive Care (HIC), Six Core Strategies, and Safewards—as practical implementations of this relational approach, aiming to reduce reliance on coercive measures while maintaining safety. Ethical and human rights considerations, particularly regarding involuntary admission under the European Convention on Human Rights and the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, are integrated throughout. The analysis concludes that sustainable safety requires aligning procedural safeguards with relational practices that foster engagement, feedback, and shared responsibility. Key Entities Joint Commission (type: org) — referenced as an organization that audits procedural safet

title: Ethics of Coercive Treatment and Misuse of Psychiatry
text: Ethics of Coercive Treatment and Misuse of Psychiatry Summary This source presents a principled ethical framework for evaluating coercive treatment in psychiatry, grounded in Beauchamp and Childress’ four principles of biomedical ethics (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) and aligned with the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UN-CRPD). The author argues that coercive treatment can be ethically justified only under strict conditions: (1) when a patient’s capacity to consent is substantially impaired, (2) when severe danger to health or life cannot be prevented by less intrusive means, and (3) when the intervention aligns with the patient’s presumed values and preferences. Crucially, coercion must never be used for behavior control without therapeutic intent—termed "misuse of psychiatry"—and a mental disorder diagnosis alone does not justify overriding a patient’s will. The paper uses clinical cases (Anna and Thomas) to illustrate how incapacity to consent, rather than diagnosis, should determine ethical permissibility, and emphasizes supported decision-making as a prerequisite before resorting to coercion. It also expands the concept of beneficence to include psychosocial health—such as social inclusion and freedom from exclusion—as a legitimate basis for intervention in cases where untreated illness leads to severe social harm. Key Entities United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons With Disabilities (UN-CRPD) (type: org) — Serves

title: Measurement of agitation and aggression in adult and aged neuropsychiatric patients review of definitions and frequently used measurement scales
text: Measurement of agitation and aggression in adult and aged neuropsychiatric patients review of definitions and frequently used measurement scales Summary This review article examines the frequent conflation of agitation and aggression in clinical and research settings, arguing that these are distinct behavioral syndromes requiring separate assessment. The authors analyze commonly used measurement scales—including the Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS), Neuropsychiatric Inventory (NPI), and Cohen-Mansfield Agitation Inventory (CMAI)—and demonstrate how these tools often combine agitation and aggression indicators in single items or subscales (e.g., NPI's "agitation/aggression" item, PANSS Excited Component). This conflation obscures differential treatment effects, as a medication may effectively target one syndrome but not the other, leading to misleading null results in clinical trials when only combined scores are used. The paper emphasizes that agitation is defined as excessive motor or verbal activity without focus or intent, while aggression involves behavior intended to cause harm, and highlights clinical observations showing that these syndromes can occur independently (e.g., pure agitation in dementia, reactive aggression triggered by resistance to care without agitation). The authors advocate for using scales that clearly separate these constructs (such as those listed in Table 2) and recommend distinct outcome measures for agitation and aggression in treatme

title: Measurement and Prevalence of Aggression
text: Measurement and Prevalence of Aggression Summary This chapter examines the measurement and prevalence of aggression in mental health settings, focusing on healthcare workplace violence from both patient and worker perspectives. It discusses the complexity of defining aggression and violence, noting multiple definitions exist with differing emphases on act type, intent, and harm. The chapter emphasizes that psychiatric settings have the highest rates of healthcare workplace violence, citing U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics data showing 124.9 per 10,000 workers in psychiatric/substance use settings versus 12.8 in all hospitals. It highlights that measurement must capture both patient aggression incidents and worker exposure, as they differ and both are important for understanding safety and intervention effectiveness. The chapter reviews various measurement tools, including observation scales like the Overt Aggression Scale (OAS) and its modifications, and incident-based tools like the Staff Observation Aggression Scale-Revised (SOAS-R), discussing their strengths and limitations. It identifies significant under-reporting as a major issue, with studies suggesting up to 70% of violent incidents go unreported due to shame, fear, and perceived futility. The chapter also details serious consequences of worker exposure to violence, including psychological distress, depression, anxiety, PTSD, decreased job satisfaction, turnover intent, and importantly, a dose-response relationship wi

title: Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions?
text: Is psychiatry torn in different ethical directions? Summary This paper examines the ethical tensions within UK mental health legislation, arguing that psychiatry is uniquely positioned in a conflict between competing ethical frameworks due to the nature of the laws governing it. The Mental Health Act 2007 (MHA 2007) is characterized as predominantly utilitarian, prioritizing outcomes such as preventing harm and promoting welfare, even when applied to individuals with full decision-making capacity. In contrast, the Human Rights Act 1998 (HRA 1998) and the Mental Capacity Act 2005 (MCA 2005) are framed as rights-focused, emphasizing the protection of autonomy and self-determination for capable individuals. The author contends that this creates a fundamental inconsistency: psychiatry can detain capable individuals based on mental health criteria under MHA 2007, while HRA 1998 and MCA 2005 protect such autonomy in all other medical contexts. No equivalent legal mechanism exists for detaining capable individuals with physical illnesses who make harmful health choices (e.g., in diabetes or COPD), highlighting a discriminatory application of utilitarian principles solely to mental illness. The paper further notes that while MCA 2005 requires capacity assessments across medicine, the frequent overlap of assessor and decision-maker roles introduces a conflict of interest, only partially addressed by the Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS), which apply to a small subset of patient

title: On the Relative Intrusiveness of Physical and Chemical Restraints
text: On the Relative Intrusiveness of Physical and Chemical Restraints Summary The article examines whether consciousness-altering chemical restraints (CACRs) are less intrusive on basic liberty than physical restraints, challenging the prevailing view that such chemical interventions are less liberty-infringing. Drawing on philosophical analysis of freedom and agency, the authors argue that while physical restraints like wrist straps restrict specific bodily movements, CACRs interfere with a wider range of mental states—including intentions, decisions, and volition—thereby potentially constituting a greater infringement on liberty. The analysis uses thought experiments comparing patients under physical restraint (who may resist but retain volition) versus those under CACRs (who comply due to altered intentions). The authors conclude that CACRs may often restrict basic liberty more extensively than physical restraints because they disrupt the formation of intentions rather than merely blocking their physical execution, suggesting a need to reassess ethical hierarchies that favor chemical over physical restraint based on perceived lesser intrusion. Key Entities Oxford University (organization): Institutional affiliation of multiple authors; center of academic and ethical inquiry behind the argument. National University of Singapore (organization): Affiliation of one author; contributes to the international perspective on psychiatric ethics. AJOB Neuroscience (journal/publication):

title: Seclusion or Restraint
text: Seclusion or Restraint Summary This resource document from the American Psychiatric Association’s Patient Safety Work Group provides practical considerations for the use of seclusion and restraint in psychiatric care for adults in emergency and hospital settings. It synthesizes clinical guidelines, legal standards, and research evidence to emphasize that seclusion and restraint should be interventions of last resort, only used after less restrictive measures fail. The document highlights the disproportionate risks associated with physical restraint—such as deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and post-traumatic stress—compared to seclusion, and underscores the importance of patient preferences, psychiatric advance directives, trauma-informed care, cultural competency, de-escalation techniques, and environmental modifications in reducing reliance on these interventions. It also addresses systemic disparities in use linked to race, socioeconomic status, and homelessness, and calls for post-incident reviews to improve equity and safety. Key Entities American Psychiatric Association (APA) (organization): The professional body that issued the resource document through its Patient Safety Work Group and Council on Quality Care to guide clinicians on seclusion and restraint practices. Patient Safety Work Group (PSWG) (organization): The APA subgroup responsible for developing this resource document, focusing on safety, ethics, and equity in psychiatric care. Council on Quality C

title: Coercion – point, perception, process
text: Coercion – point, perception, process Summary This study examines the experience of coercion among individuals with serious mental illness under New York's Kendra's Law, which mandates assisted outpatient treatment (AOT). Researchers compared perceived coercion between those court-ordered into AOT and those receiving voluntary outpatient treatment (OPT), using the Perceived Coercion Scale (PCS) alongside qualitative interviews. Contrary to expectations, no significant difference in perceived coercion was found between groups. The PCS demonstrated strong reliability and validity, correlating with poor insight, prior involuntary hospitalizations, stigma, poor therapeutic alliance, and low quality of life. Qualitative analysis revealed three key themes: the ubiquity of prior coercive experiences making court orders feel incremental, a tendency to choose compliance over confrontation, and the valuing of services like housing and case management that can reframe coercion as supportive. The findings suggest that subjective coercion is shaped more by relational context and service access than by legal status alone. Key Entities National Institutes of Health, National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (organization) — Affiliation of lead author; provided institutional support for the study. Albert Einstein College of Medicine (organization) — Affiliation of second author; contributed to study design and implementation. Columbia University, New York State Psychiatric

title: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights
text: Mental Capacity, Dignity and the Power of International Human Rights Summary This chapter examines the principle of indivisibility of human rights and its application to Article 12 of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), which concerns the right to legal capacity. It traces the historical development of the indivisibility principle within the United Nations human rights framework, from the split between the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) and the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR) to its affirmation in instruments like the Vienna Declaration. The author argues that indivisibility comprises two interrelated concepts: the interdependence of rights (where the realization of one depends on others) and the rejection of any hierarchy between civil-political and socio-economic rights. Applying this to disability rights, the chapter contends that the right to legal capacity under Article 12 cannot be understood in isolation from the right to support, as the two are interdependent—legal capacity often depends on access to support services, which constitute socio-economic rights. Consequently, for some individuals with cognitive disability who face socio-economic disadvantages due to the residual impact of impairment, realizing legal capacity may require support measures, including substitute decision-making as a last resort. This challenges interpretations that view Article 12 as requiring t

title: Proportionality and Absolute Rights
text: Proportionality and Absolute Rights Summary Grégoire Webber's paper examines the relationship between absolute rights and the principle of proportionality in human rights law. It critiques two dominant views held by proportionality proponents: that proportionality does not apply to absolute rights, or that absolute rights are merely predictable outcomes of proportionality analysis. Webber argues both views stem from a flawed conception of rights as individual interests rather than relational duties. Through comparative analysis of human rights instruments like the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, European Convention on Human Rights, and national bills of rights, he demonstrates that absolute rights (e.g., prohibitions against torture and slavery) are defined by negative injunctions that specify duties of non-action ("no one shall torture"). This relational structure—where rights correlate directly to duties owed by others—grounds their absoluteness in interpersonal obligations, not formal legal features or probabilistic balancing. The paper concludes that proper specification of rights as duties between persons is necessary to realize their normative force and defend them against reductive interest-based theories. Key Entities Universal Declaration of Human Rights (document) — Provides dual formulations of rights (affirmative and negative) used to illustrate how negative injunctions explicitly tie rights to duties of non-action. European Convention on Human Rights (docu

title: Coercive Measures in Psychiatry: A Review of Ethical Arguments
text: Coercive Measures in Psychiatry: A Review of Ethical Arguments Summary This narrative review by Chieze et al. (2021) systematically analyzes ethical arguments for and against coercive measures in psychiatry, focusing on interventions that restrict freedom of movement: seclusion, physical restraint, and involuntary hospitalization. The authors searched multiple databases (PubMed, Embase, PsycINFO, Google Scholar, Cairn.info) using keywords related to coercion, psychiatry, and ethics, ultimately analyzing 99 articles in English, French, and German that addressed ethical aspects of formal coercion in adult psychiatric inpatient settings. Their qualitative analysis identified four main ethical categories: arguments for absolute prohibition (non-negotiable rejection), conditions under which coercion might be acceptable (both supporting and opposing factors), and procedural guidelines for ethical decision-making. The review finds that while some argue coercion always violates inviolable rights like autonomy and dignity, the majority view holds that coercion can be ethically justified in exceptional circumstances when it serves higher-order values—such as preventing harm, enabling long-term recovery of autonomy, or preserving therapeutic relationships—provided that rigorous procedural safeguards (proportionality, necessity, last resort) are followed and a transparent balancing of competing values occurs. The authors emphasize that ethical justification requires more than legal compl

title: Physical Restraint: A Historical Review and Current Practice
text: Physical Restraint: A Historical Review and Current Practice Summary This article traces the 300-year history of physical restraint in psychiatric care, beginning with its justification under English vagrancy laws and the quid pro quo principle, through reform efforts by figures like Philippe Pinel and John Conolly, to 20th-century transformations driven by the patient rights movement and landmark litigation such as O’Connor v. Donaldson, Youngberg v. Romeo, and Wyatt v. Stickney. It highlights how growing evidence of harm—particularly the 1998 Hartford Courant report linking 140 deaths to restraint over a decade, many due to asphyxiation in children—prompted regulatory action by the Joint Commission and Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS). These agencies established strict standards limiting restraint to immediate danger scenarios, requiring continuous monitoring, prohibiting PRN orders, and mandating debriefing and data collection. The article further outlines prevention strategies including staff training in de-escalation, patient and family involvement, environmental modifications like comfort rooms, and specific models such as collaborative problem solving and trauma-informed care, positioning restraint as a last resort rather than a routine intervention. Key Entities Joint Commission (organization): Accrediting body that established guidelines for restraint and seclusion use in healthcare facilities, requiring monitoring, training, and data reporting. Cente

title: Structured assessments for imminent aggression in mental health and correctional settings: Systematic review and meta-analysis
text: Structured assessments for imminent aggression in mental health and correctional settings: Systematic review and meta-analysis Summary This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the predictive validity of structured risk assessment tools for imminent aggression (defined as violence likely within 24 hours of assessment) in mental health and correctional settings. The authors identified 32 relevant instruments but found only five with sufficient research: the Brøset Violence Checklist (BVC), Dynamic Appraisal of Situational Aggression (DASA) and its youth/women variants, and the HCR-20 clinical subscale. A narrative review of 50 papers and meta-analysis of 31 studies (282,238 assessments from 2,723 individuals) revealed that only BVC and DASA had adequate evidence for meaningful conclusions. After removing outliers, both tools showed large effect sizes for predicting person-directed aggression (BVC: Hedges' g = 1.35; DASA: g = 1.04) and any aggression (BVC: g = 1.24; DASA: g = 0.88). Moderator analyses indicated that effect sizes decreased in lower bias-risk studies and increased in forensic versus civil settings. Implementation studies consistently reported both tools as acceptable and feasible for clinical use, though concerns were noted about duplicated data collection and inconsistent real-world application due to insufficient interrater reliability reporting in many studies. title: Psychiatry and the law – do the fields agree in their views on coercive treatment?
text: Psychiatry and the law – do the fields agree in their views on coercive treatment? Summary This chapter examines the intersection of psychiatry and law regarding coercive treatment, arguing that coercion is not merely a tool but a defining, inherent element of psychiatric practice across historical and contemporary contexts. It traces coercion from historical asylum practices through deinstitutionalization to modern community-based mechanisms like community treatment orders (CTOs) and "troffers" (threat-offer hybrids). The author contends that psychiatry and law converge on the legitimacy of coercion only when it adheres to principles of necessity, proportionality, and last resort, grounded in a balance between individual rights (both negative and positive) and public safety. The analysis integrates legal frameworks, ethical guidelines (WHO, WPA declarations), and sociological concepts such as vulnerability and the Penrose Law to illustrate how coercion evolves but persists as a structural feature of mental health systems. Key Entities Queen’s University (org): Affiliation of the author, Julio Arboleda-Flórez, indicating the academic origin of the analysis. John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. (org): Publisher of the edited volume in which this chapter appears, responsible for dissemination. World Health Organization (org): Cited for its ethical guidelines influencing international standards on coercion in psychiatry. Pan American Health Organization (org): Referenced for the Declaration

title: Staff Training Reduces the Use of Physical Restraint in Mental Health Service, Evidence-based Reflection for China
text: Staff Training Reduces the Use of Physical Restraint in Mental Health Service, Evidence-based Reflection for China Summary This study evaluates the impact of staff training programs on reducing the use of physical restraint in mental health services in China. Using a quasi-experimental design, the research compares restraint use before and after implementing structured training interventions focused on de-escalation techniques, patient-centered care, and legal/ethical guidelines. Results show a statistically significant decrease in physical restraint incidents following training, suggesting that targeted education can effectively alter clinical practices. The findings emphasize the role of organizational commitment and sustained training in achieving lasting reductions in coercive measures, offering a model for improving mental health care standards through evidence-based staff development. title: Current trends in restrictive interventions in psychiatry; a European perspective
text: Current trends in restrictive interventions in psychiatry; a European perspective Summary This article reviews current evidence on the use of coercive measures—including seclusion, physical, mechanical, and pharmacological restraint—in psychiatric inpatient settings across Europe. It synthesizes data from multiple countries to describe patterns of use, legal and policy frameworks, clinical indications, consequences, and strategies to reduce reliance on such interventions. The review places particular emphasis on mechanical restraint due to its prolonged duration and unique ethical challenges. The authors aim to clarify when restrictive interventions may be necessary, examine their use in specialist settings (e.g., forensic psychiatry, dementia care), and evaluate current European laws and policies governing seclusion and restraint. The research design is a narrative review of empirical studies, systematic reviews, and policy documents up to 2022. Findings reveal substantial cross-national variation in reported rates: highest in Poland, Greece, and Italy; lowest in Iceland, where seclusion and mechanical restraint were abolished approximately 30 years ago. Within countries like Germany and Denmark, usage differs significantly between forensic and general psychiatry settings, and even among individual hospitals. Mechanical restraint is more common in forensic contexts in Germany (21.2% seclusion vs. 3.2% mechanical restraint), while the reverse is true in general psychiatry. Tr

title: The constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medications
text: The constitutional right to refuse antipsychotic medications Summary This article analyzes the emergence of a constitutional right for involuntarily hospitalized mental patients to refuse antipsychotic medications, focusing on two pivotal federal cases: Rennie v. Klein (New Jersey) and Rogers v. Okin (Massachusetts). The author, Alexander D. Brooks, examines how courts have grounded this right in constitutional doctrines—primarily the right to privacy, with supplementary reliance on First Amendment protections in Rogers—while establishing that the right is not absolute and may be overridden when a patient is deemed incompetent or dangerous. The piece details the procedural remedies crafted by each court: Rogers mandating judicial hearings with appointed guardians, and Reeves establishing an in-hospital review process involving independent psychiatrists and patient advocates. Beyond legal analysis, Brooks presents substantial clinical evidence regarding the risks of antipsychotics, emphasizing tardive dyskinesia as a potentially irreversible and underrecognized iatrogenic harm, alongside other side effects like akathisia, akinesia, and cognitive dulling. The article critiques psychiatric resistance to acknowledging these harms, documenting instances of institutional denial (e.g., at Ancora State Hospital) where chart reviews failed to detect tardive dyskinesia despite clinical evidence, and highlights systemic issues such as diagnostic overshadowing and the therapeutic misconc

title: The Legitimisation of Psychiatric Measures Against a Person's Will
text: The Legitimisation of Psychiatric Measures Against a Person's Will Summary This chapter traces the historical evolution of justifications for coercive psychiatric practices, from the institution-centered phase focused on social control and public order to the person-centered phase grounded in clinical-ethical-legal frameworks. During the institution-centered era, coercion was justified through doctrines like parens patriae and concerns about family reputation or public nuisance, often without psychiatric input and under inhumane conditions. The shift toward person-centered care from the 1970s/1980s introduced medical and ethical criteria such as diagnosed mental illness, risk of harm, last resort, and least restrictive alternative, as reflected in guidelines from bodies like the German Ethics Council and European surveys. Despite this evolution, the chapter argues that coercion remains ethically contested, with tensions between autonomy and beneficence, and highlights ongoing debates about its efficacy, particularly regarding claims that it fosters insight or recovery. The analysis underscores a persistent "clinical-ethical-legal stalemate," exemplified by the Geneva impasse in WHO/UN forums, where competing human rights frameworks prevent consensus on abolition versus regulation of coercion. Key Entities World Health Organisation (WHO) (org): Developer of the International Classification of Diseases (ICD), which standardizes mental disorder classifications globally and influ

title: Effectiveness of De-Escalation in Reducing Aggression and Coercion in Acute Psychiatric Units. A Cluster Randomized Study
text: Effectiveness of De-Escalation in Reducing Aggression and Coercion in Acute Psychiatric Units. A Cluster Randomized Study Summary This cluster randomized controlled trial evaluated the effectiveness of staff de-escalation training in reducing aggression and coercive measures across all psychiatric hospitals in Slovenia. The study compared baseline (5 months in 2018) and intervention (5 months in 2019) periods, with hospitals randomized to either receive de-escalation training (experimental) or continue treatment as usual (control). The intervention consisted of a 16-hour multimodal training program based on the Beta Project of the American Association for Emergency Psychiatry, WHO Quality Rights training, and the Amdur et al. handbook, covering verbal and non-verbal techniques. Primary outcomes included incidence of aggressive incidents (measured via the Revised Staff Observation Aggression Scale, SOAS-R), severe aggression (SOAS-R ≥9), and physical restraint use, particularly aggression-related restraints. The study included 6,401 patient episodes across 6 hospitals, demonstrating robust real-world applicability. Key findings showed that de-escalation training significantly reduced aggressive incidents by 73% (IRR=0.268), severe aggression by 86% (IRR=0.142), and aggression-related physical restraint use by 70% (IRR=0.304) in the experimental group compared to controls. The intervention also reduced the proportion of patients with recurrent aggression (from 7.6% to 1.9%) and

title: How right is right-based mental health law?
text: How right is right-based mental health law? Summary This article evaluates India's Mental Healthcare Act 2017 (MHCA 2017) through the lens of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD), assessing whether the legislation successfully implements a human rights-based approach to mental health care. The authors analyze the evolution of mental health law in India, the principles of a rights-based framework, and the alignment between MHCA 2017 and international human rights instruments, particularly the UNCRPD. They find that MHCA 2017 represents a significant advancement by guaranteeing rights such as access to care, community living, dignity, equality, and legal remedies, reflecting core UNCRPD provisions. However, the article highlights tensions in implementation, especially concerning the UNCRPD’s Article 12 on equal legal capacity, which challenges traditional notions of incapacity and may conflict with clinical practices involving substitute decision-making. The authors note that while the UNCRPD promotes autonomy and dignity, it does not adequately address the fluctuating vulnerability inherent in mental illness, and its lack of a clear definition of disability creates ambiguity about whether psychosocial disabilities are fully covered. The piece concludes that although MHCA 2017 reflects a progressive shift toward rights-based care, realizing its potential requires resolving conceptual gaps in the UNCRPD and addressing practical barrie

title: Implementing Alternatives to Coercion: A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care
text: Implementing Alternatives to Coercion: A Key Component of Improving Mental Health Care Summary This position statement by the World Psychiatric Association (WPA) examines the overuse of coercive practices in mental health care globally and advocates for the implementation of evidence-based alternatives. Coercion is defined broadly to include involuntary treatment, seclusion, restraint, and threats, and is shown to violate fundamental human rights such as liberty, autonomy, and bodily integrity. The document synthesizes research, ethical guidelines, and real-world examples to argue that reducing coercion is both feasible and essential for improving mental health care quality, particularly through frameworks like the WHO Quality Rights Initiative, Safewards, and the Six Core Strategies. It emphasizes the role of psychiatrists as leaders in driving systemic change toward recovery-oriented, trauma-informed, and rights-based care. Key Entities World Psychiatric Association (WPA) (type: org) — Issued the position statement and leads advocacy for reducing coercion in mental health care globally. World Health Organization (WHO) (type: org) — Developed the Quality Rights Initiative, a key framework cited for reducing coercion through human rights-based training and assessment. Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists (RANZCP) (type: org) — Collaborated with WPA on producing related resources and supporting implementation efforts.
